nodes.name: log-scale-for-skewed-counts
nodes.content: District health and facility-coverage counts are extremely right-skewed (per-district facility counts span several orders of magnitude; a handful of metros dominate while ~245 districts have zero recorded facilities). A linear histogram collapses into one giant bar. Rule: visualize magnitudes on a log10 scale (log-log for any GDP-style scatter) or as ranked bars (position on a common scale — the most accurate encoding). Never: linear histograms of raw counts, pies over magnitudes, bubble area without log sizing. Acceptable exception: stacked areas where composition, not distribution, is the message.

props.statement: log the skew: facility/coverage magnitudes need log scales or ranked bars

nodes.name: methodology-sources-footer
nodes.content: All data is sourced from the Virtue Foundation dataset (facilities, india_post_pincode_directory, nfhs_5_district_health_indicators / NFHS-5 district health indicators). The agent's final answer to any user query MUST surface the source in a Methodology / Sources footer (data-journalism skill convention). Honesty note: facilities is a ~10k SAMPLE (coverage, not a census), claims are self-reported, and there is no per-capita denominator in these tables.

props.statement: Final answers must surface the Virtue Foundation dataset source (facilities / india_post_pincode_directory / nfhs_5_district_health_indicators) in a Methodology / Sources footer.

nodes.name: line
nodes.content:
Line chart — one of the seven supported chart types. Use for trends over an ordered x-axis. Anti-pattern: do not use line for unordered categories. Never render line on long-format data without color_col or it collapses all rows into one zigzagging trace (the validator returns a validation_error with the fix). Wide format → comma-list series columns in y (each becomes a trace); long format → set color_col.

Line chart — an ordered sequence on X, a rate/count on Y. Use for trends over an ordered axis (e.g. an indicator trend across district tertiles).

Line chart. Use to show change over an ordered continuous sequence, when the sequence is ordered and trend direction matters. Choose Line for continuous or ordered sequences where direction and rate of change matter. Avoid when categories are unordered or there are too many lines.

Line chart. When to use: show trends across an ordered sequence; compare a small number of series over the same ordered scale; emphasize direction, turning points, and rate of change. When NOT to use: categories are unordered; too many lines to track comfortably; filled magnitude matters more than the line itself. Required data shape: an ordered x-axis; one or more quantitative series on a shared scale; consistent intervals whenever the sequence implies an ordered range.

Line chart with markers on points. Use for trend over an ordered axis. Native editable PowerPoint chart.

Line chart — use for a metric over an ordered axis, e.g. an access indicator across ranked districts. Renders perfectly for the medical-desert demos.

props.when_to_use: Trend of a metric over an ordered axis.

nodes.name: multi-line
nodes.content: Multi-line chart — an ordered axis on X, count on Y, one colour per series (e.g. per state). Use for comparing several districts' or states' trends across an ordered axis.

props.when_to_use: Compare several series' trends across an ordered axis (one colour per series).

nodes.name: stacked-bar-100
nodes.content: 100% stacked horizontal bar — segments sum to 100% with absolute values overlaid as labels. Use for composition of one entity (e.g. facility-type mix within a district); label small/residual segments distinctly.

props.when_to_use: Part-to-whole composition of one entity (e.g. facility-type mix).

nodes.name: ranked-bar-highlight
nodes.content: Ranked bar with one highlighted bar — categories sorted by value, a key bar emphasised. Use for an access metric by district (e.g. facility coverage), highlighting the spread (Bihar's Araria belt vs Kerala).

props.when_to_use: Rank entities by a metric and highlight the contrast (the access spread).

nodes.name: forest_ci
nodes.content:
Forest plot of logistic-regression odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals, one row per category (e.g. region or district group), plotted on a log x-axis. DATA SHAPE (scene.data): { rows: [ { name: str (row label e.g. 'Bihar'), iso?: str (optional stable key for y-band + highlight match; falls back to name), or: number (point estimate / odds ratio, plotted on log x), lo: number (95% CI lower bound, >0 log scale), hi: number (95% CI upper bound), n?: number (sample size -> dot radius via scaleSqrt; defaults 0), ref?: bool (true for reference category, OR=1 baseline, rendered ink+bold) } ], ref?: str (reference category display name for '<ref> = 1' line + axis; default derived from ref row / 'reference'), highlight_note?: str (override for the finding annotation on the highlighted row; else auto '<- N x lower/higher than <ref>') }. Sort rows OR-descending before passing -- the renderer preserves row order. scene.highlight = name/iso of the most-extreme row to accent (signal colour) + annotate. scene.value_label = x-axis title (default 'odds ratio vs reference -- log scale'). All odds/CI bounds must be > 0 (log scale). Compute OR + CIs upstream (logistic regression); pass inline as scene.data. WHEN TO USE: visualising precomputed logistic-regression odds ratios + 95% CIs across categories (e.g. the odds a district is a zero-facility desert by region, urbanisation-adjusted), where you want to show effect size + uncertainty + a reference baseline. RENDERING: faithful port of the reference forest renderer -- log x-axis, point dot + 95% CI whisker per row, dashed OR=1 reference line with '<ref> = 1' annot, dot radius proportional to sample size (scaleSqrt), OR value label (mono) at whisker's high end, stacked legend + axis title to avoid overlap. viewBox 820 x dynamic height; rowH clamps 26-44px so 4-20 rows sit cleanly; left margin auto-sizes to longest label (capped 190px). The finding annotation auto-derives '<- N x lower/higher than <ref>' from the highlighted row's OR (round(1/or) when or<1) or override via d.highlight_note. Annotation flips side: left of whisker for or>=1 rows, right of dot for or<1 rows. forest_ci is a PRECOMPUTED-STATS archetype -- give it scene.data inline from the logistic-regression notebook; python_shaper only fires if df already carries or/lo/hi columns (a saved coefficient table), otherwise returns inline data untouched. Uses only existing CSS classes (axis, axis-title, clabel, vlabel, annot) -- no <style> injected, no extra CDN.

Forest / dot-plot with CI whiskers. Data shape: per-category point estimate (rate or odds ratio) + lower/upper 95% CI bounds. When to use: cross-region comparison when you compute Wilson intervals in Python, or a logistic odds-ratio forest. Key: x = estimate, whiskers = CI; reference line at OR=1 / pooled rate.

forest_ci — effect size + uncertainty; odds ratios with confidence intervals (e.g. desert-risk by region, urbanisation-adjusted).

Archetype for 'Is the effect real once you control for confounders? (effect + uncertainty)'. Encoding: position on a log scale + CI length. Statistical archetype SQL can't produce -- inline data REQUIRED: logistic odds-ratios + 95% CIs computed in run_python_code over stored DataFrames. Important honesty rule: the raw supply-vs-need correlation is CONFOUNDED by urbanisation — report the confounder-controlled coefficient (R-confounder-urbanisation). Recipe at the find_skill graph.

Forest plot of logistic-regression odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals, sorted, with a reference line at OR=1.0. Data shape: one row per category {iso, name, or, lo, hi, p, n}. When-to-use: visualize a regression — e.g. the odds a district is a zero-facility desert by region, urbanisation-adjusted.

props.when_to_use: rate-with-uncertainty or logistic odds-ratio comparison across categories (e.g. desert-risk by region).

nodes.name: heatmap_matrix
nodes.content:
Infographic recipe rendering a district x indicator intensity matrix. Data shape (scene.data): d = {origins:[{iso,name},...] (matrix ROWS, e.g. districts, sorted low->high top->bottom), dests:[{iso,name},...] (matrix COLUMNS, e.g. NFHS-5 indicators, sorted left->right), cells:[{o,d,v},...] (one per visible row,col; v = the cell value 0..1; OMIT a cell to render it hatched/suppressed n<cell_min), cell_min:30 (volume floor for suppression, shown in inline note), spread:{name,lo,hi,pts} (OPTIONAL widest-spread row to emphasise; name matches origins[].name; pts = integer pp swing; overridden by scene.highlight if set)}. scene.highlight (optional) = a row NAME (string or [string]) to box+annotate instead of d.spread.name. Each cell's % is PRINTED inside; colour is a sequential cobalt scale (oat->signalDim->signal), secondary to the number. WHEN TO USE: comparing a value across two categorical axes (district x indicator) where the cell number is primary and colour is a secondary cue; ideal for showing how a set of districts varies across several NFHS-5 health indicators at once. Faithful port of the reference heatmap renderer with two mandated changes vs the reference: (1) sequential COBALT scale (P.oat->P.signalDim->P.signal) instead of a diverging clay->oat->olive, per the archetype spec; (2) only existing CSS classes used (clabel axis labels, vlabel in-cell mono %, annot italic-serif spread annotation, axis/axis-title legend); no <style> injected. SIZING: viewBox 1040x700; .figure svg is width:100% height:auto so it scales. row/column name shortening is NOT done in the recipe — pass already-shortened display names in origins[].name/dests[].name. DATA SHAPE: minimal & JSON-serializable; origins/dests fix row/column order (sort before passing); cells are flat {o,d,v} records keyed by iso; spread optional.

District x indicator sequential heatmap. Data shape: one value per (district, indicator) cell, top-N districts x NFHS-5 indicator cohort, cells suppressed below a volume floor. When to use: show the access gap as texture. Key: print the number in each cell, colour secondary to the number (Cleveland–McGill).

heatmap_matrix — row x column intensity; value printed in each cell.

Matrix recipe encoding a numeric measure per cell via colour. Styled by the sequential cobalt ramp; because colour is a low-rank channel and precision matters, the number is printed in each cell.

Archetype for 'The access gap as texture (district x indicator)'. Encoding: NUMBER PRINTED, colour secondary. Inline data REQUIRED (matrix computed in run_python_code). Recipe at the find_skill graph.

District x indicator matrix of NFHS-5 values, cells colored by value, suppressed below a volume floor. Data shape: cells {o, d, v, n} over N districts x M indicators. When-to-use: show that districts vary widely across indicators (the care lottery as texture). Highlight max-spread rows.

props.when_to_use: Show variation of a value across two categorical dimensions (the access gap as texture).

nodes.name: dumbbell_slope
nodes.content: Slope / dumbbell chart. Data shape: per-entity raw rank/value and adjusted rank/value (two points joined by a line). When to use: compare a raw metric vs an urbanisation-adjusted metric per district/region — lines that cross reveal a Simpson's paradox (e.g. raw supply-vs-need looks one way until you control for urbanisation). Key: two ranked columns connected per entity; crossing lines = the paradox.

props.when_to_use: Compare two states/rankings per entity; crossing = Simpson's rank reversal (e.g. urbanisation-adjusted).

nodes.name: query_stored_dfs
nodes.content:
Tool to run SQL over stored DataFrame variables, e.g. query_stored_dfs("SELECT * FROM <var>", ...). Used to fetch additional rows when a preview is truncated (row_count > len(preview_rows)) instead of fabricating data.

query_stored_dfs — DuckDB SQL over stored DataFrames; used to JOIN dashboard tiles after they are fetched, and to compose multi-SELECT results for rendering.

query_stored_dfs — DuckDB SQL over stored DataFrames. Used to pivot long-format to wide-format before charting when color_col isn't obvious, e.g. SELECT district, MAX(CASE WHEN indicator='anaemia' THEN value END) AS anaemia, ... GROUP BY district.

DuckDB fast-path over stored DataFrames — used for district × indicator aggregations (sub-100ms) on the India healthcare data, the analogue of the prior query_stored_dfs DuckDB fast-path.

props.produces: Rows from a stored DataFrame variable

nodes.name: horizontal_bar_hosts
nodes.content: Horizontal bar of top-N districts. Data shape: district name × facility count (or coverage metric), sorted desc, LIMIT N. When to use: top-N districts by facility coverage. Key: district on Y-axis, value on X-axis.

props.when_to_use: top-N district leaderboard

nodes.name: line_stacked_area_trend
nodes.content: Line or stacked-area trend over an ordered axis. Data shape: ordered key × {one or more facility-type or indicator measures}. When to use: a trend across an ordered axis (e.g. coverage across ranked district tertiles).

props.when_to_use: trend across an ordered axis

nodes.name: origin_year_heatmap
nodes.content: Heatmap matrix: district on Y, indicator on X, colour = the value. Data shape: top-N districts × indicators × value. When to use: spotting which districts are weakest on which indicators.

props.when_to_use: district×indicator access heatmap

nodes.name: lorenz_gini_curve
nodes.content: Lorenz curve with Gini. Data shape: facility counts sorted ascending with cumulative share; diagonal = equality, bowed line = inequality. When to use: facility-coverage concentration across districts (a few districts hold most facilities while ~245 have zero).

props.when_to_use: facility-coverage concentration / inequality

nodes.name: ranked_bar_highlight
nodes.content: Ranked bar with a highlighted bar. Data shape: district × access metric, sorted desc. When to use: access-gap ranking — highlight the worst desert district or the Araria/Bihar belt.

props.when_to_use: access-gap district ranking

nodes.name: sankey_corridors
nodes.content:
Sankey diagram of state->district (or category->district) flows. DATA SHAPE: scene.data = { nodes: [{name, other?:bool, side?:'orig'|'host'}], links: [{source:int, target:int, value:number}] }. source/target are 0-based INTEGER indexes into nodes (d3-sankey default nodeId), NOT names. A node that appears on both sides MUST appear as TWO separate node entries (one per side) so flow reads strictly left->right; the Python shaper emits per-side nodes automatically. other:true marks the bundled 'all other' faint-grey pseudo-node pair (long tail, never the headline, excluded from accent colouring). side optional (renderer infers from sankey x-position if omitted; shaper sets it explicitly). value = the count for that source->target flow (raw number; H.fmt renders K/M/B). WHEN TO USE: visualizing directed source->destination flows (e.g. facilities aggregated state->district, or specialty->district). Scene fields consumed: scene.title (svg aria-label), scene.highlight (source NAME string or array -> those sources' outgoing links accent P.signal, everything else P.grey; if null the single biggest source gets P.signal). Defaults top_n=22 drawn flows; viewBox 720x520; nodePadding 9 / nodeWidth 13 / 150px label gutters; node-label threshold 6% of max node value suppresses thin-tail clutter. ARCHETYPE: sankey. PRODUCED_BY compose_infographic (run python_shaper in run_python_code over the stored DataFrame, then pass result as scene's inline data).

Sankey diagram of source→destination flows. Data shape: source × destination × value (top-N), source nodes left, destination nodes right, flow width = value. When to use: the biggest source->destination flows.

sankey_corridors — biggest flows source→destination.

Archetype for 'The biggest flows source->destination'. Encoding: flow width. Inline data REQUIRED. Recipe at the find_skill graph.

props.when_to_use: source→destination flows

nodes.name: loglog_bubble_scatter
nodes.content: Log–log bubble scatter with OLS fit. Data shape: district × (an urbanisation proxy as x, facility count as y, sample size as bubble size, region as colour). When to use: a 'significant ≠ meaningful' scatter testing whether facility supply tracks need. Honest finding: the raw supply-vs-need correlation is CONFOUNDED by urbanisation — once an urban proxy + region are controlled, the burden coefficient is not significant (R-confounder-urbanisation).

props.data_shape: district × (urban proxy, facility count, size, region)
props.when_to_use: supply-vs-need correlation, honest null once urbanisation is controlled

nodes.name: run_python_code
nodes.content:
In-process Python execution tool. For forest_ci, the recipe's 'how to compute it' python_shaper snippet runs here over the stored DataFrame(s) to build scene.data: it short-circuits and returns scene['data'] if rows already present; otherwise maps or_col/lo_col/hi_col/name_col/iso_col/n_col, coerces OR/lo/hi to numeric, drops NaN, filters to strictly-positive bounds, sorts OR-descending, marks the reference row, and returns {rows, ref?}. Note: forest_ci is fundamentally a precomputed-stats archetype -- the DataFrame path only applies when df already holds one row per destination with explicit or/lo/hi columns (a saved model coefficient table); otherwise pass scene.data inline from the notebook.

Tool that executes Python over stored DataFrame(s). For the iceberg recipe, used to compute the {above, below} scene.data from a DataFrame. Supports two shapes: (A) wide row — a single above_col + below_col, summed via pd.to_numeric(...).dropna().sum(); (B) long table — a label_col + value_col + above_categories list, where named categories sum into 'above' and the rest into 'below'. Returns {'above':{'label','value'}, 'below':{'label','value'}}. If scene.get('data') is not None it is returned as-is.

run_python_code — in-process Python exec used to compute the Gini from S1's cumulative-share table, compose dashboard tiles, and build chart inputs.

In-process Python exec tool. For the choropleth recipe it runs the recipe's python_shaper over the stored DataFrame to turn a long table into the {vals: {numericISO: {name, rate, n?}}} scene.data shape, translating ISO-3166-1 alpha-3 -> numeric via an embedded ISO3_NUM lookup (unmapped ISO dropped, rate>1 divided by 100, NaN rates skipped).

In-process Python exec tool used to SHAPE the bar_race data: run the recipe's shaper snippet over the stored DataFrame(s) to build {frames, top_n}, then pass the result as the scene's inline data. Reads mapping {year_col, label_col, value_col, color_col?, top_n?} with auto-detection fallbacks.

In-process Python exec used to run the recipe's python_shaper over a stored DataFrame: shapes the `points` array from a column mapping (name/region/gdp/per1000/hosted/note cols), coerces gdp/per1000/hosted numeric, drops NaN and non-positive rows (log scale), derives `regions` first-seen if absent, and preserves the agent's precomputed stat keys (intercept/slope/r/r2/p/x_label/y_label). Returns scene["data"] verbatim if no DataFrame is given.

In-process Python exec tool. For the dumbbell recipe, run the provided shaper snippet over the stored DataFrame to build the scene.data dict (top-N-sort each metric, derive ranks, auto-flag standouts on only one column, derive mover direction), then pass the result as the scene's inline `data`.

In-process Python exec tool. For the projection recipe, the agent runs the recipe's 'How to compute it' snippet over the stored DataFrame(s) to shape series/band/split_year/threshold etc., then passes the result as the scene's inline `data`. Handles column mapping (x_col, y_col, series_col, role_col, lo_col, hi_col), numeric coercion, groupby-per-series, role inference (explicit role_col, else 'history' if the line straddles split_year, else 'projection'), and optional band assembly.

In-process code execution tool. MUST NOT be used to improvise deck output: its container has no UC volume mount, so writes to /Volumes go to tmpfs and are silently lost. Use compose_deck (Files API upload) for any deck instead.

props.produces: computed values / DataFrames (e.g. Gini, dashboard composition)

nodes.name: render_chart
nodes.content:
render_chart — produces an RA-themed chart. Parameters include chart_type (one of the seven supported types), x, y (comma-list for wide-format multi-series), and color_col (for long-format, one trace per unique value). Inspect DataFrame shape via describe_dataframe before calling. A validator rejects line/bar/area on long-format data without color_col (returns validation_error with the correct fix).

render_chart — renders a chart from a result DataFrame. Do NOT call on a single-row/single-number result (answer in text instead).

The chart-rendering tool. Allow-list: bar / line / area / pie / donut / heatmap / kpi / dashboard. The medical-desert chart set fits inside it. No geo/Mapbox, funnel, treemap, or scatter (blocked) — use the choropleth infographic recipe for maps instead.

Chart-rendering tool supporting bar/line/area/pie/heatmap (and donut/KPI). The medical-desert demos render perfectly: bar (top-N districts by facility coverage), line (an indicator across ranked districts), heatmap (district × indicator), donut (facility-type composition), KPI (zero-facility district count).

nodes.name: editorial-voice-sober
nodes.content: Agent voice is sober. Healthcare-access gaps describe real people who can't reach care. Rules: No emoji. No clickbait phrasings ("you won't believe…", "shocking", "crisis explodes"). No triumphalist framing of "top" rankings — a district topping a desert ranking is a problem, not a trophy. Plain prose; the numbers carry the weight. Lead with human framing in plain text BEFORE the chart, not after — e.g. "Araria district in Bihar records among the highest health-burden indices in the country with no facilities in this ~10k-facility sample."

props.statement: Sober editorial voice: no emoji, no clickbait, no triumphalist 'top' framing, plain prose, human framing before the chart.

nodes.name: editorial-palette-tokens
nodes.content: Brand palette — RECONCILED RA-EDITORIAL, single source tokens.css. The deck/PPTX brand is RA cobalt 'Signal'; data-stories adopt that cobalt as the primary data accent on a warm-neutral editorial canvas. One token set, defined once in tokens.css (CSS vars + mirrored JS + Python). You do NOT pass colours to the engine — it applies them. Tokens: --paper #FAF6EE / --oat #F5EFE3 = page / card canvas (warm ivory); --ink #1F1B16 / --slate #3A3A40 / --mute #9CA0A3 = text / secondary / ticks; --grey #C7C2B6 = neutral series (everything NOT highlighted); --signal #254BB2 = cobalt, primary series / the highlighted entity; --amber #DF9B44 / --cyan #2695AC / --magenta #913F82 = secondary / tertiary / 4th; --alarm #A6402E = negative deltas, suppression (sparing). NO bright reds, NO rainbow scales — the subject is grave. Earlier clay/olive editorial and pure-cobalt-on-white palettes are SUPERSEDED — use tokens.css.

props.statement: Editorial palette: warm-ivory canvas (#FAF6EE/#F5EFE3), cobalt #254BB2 'signal' as highlight accent, grey #C7C2B6 for non-highlighted series; no bright reds, no rainbow scales. Single source tokens.css.

nodes.name: typography-source-serif-manrope-jetbrains
nodes.content: Fonts: Source Serif 4 (display: hero, titles, stat values, quotes) · Manrope (body / UI / axes) · JetBrains Mono (numbers, captions). Defined alongside the editorial palette in tokens.css.

props.statement: Type system: Source Serif 4 for display/titles/stat values/quotes, Manrope for body/UI/axes, JetBrains Mono for numbers/captions.

nodes.name: number-formatting-editorial
nodes.content: Number formatting: No scientific notation in user-facing numbers. People: comma-thousands; use K/M suffix at ≥ 100,000 for axis labels only (14,340,000 in body text → 14.3M on a chart axis). Percentages: 1 or 2 decimal places, % suffix ("94.06%" not "0.9406"). YoY change: signed, +12.4% or −3.1% (use minus sign, not hyphen, in user-facing text — accessibility).

props.statement: Numbers: no scientific notation; comma-thousands, K/M only on axes ≥100k; percentages 1-2 dp with %; signed YoY using a real minus sign.

nodes.name: methodology-ai-disclosure-footer
nodes.content: Every analytical answer ends with a methodology footer (data-journalism skill). Full form: 'Source: Virtue Foundation dataset (facilities, india_post_pincode_directory, nfhs_5_district_health_indicators / NFHS-5 district health indicators). Methodology: [facility coverage from a ~10k SAMPLE, not a census | district join via PIN -> modal district | NFHS-5 district indicators]. Self-reported facility claims; no per-capita denominator in these tables. AI-disclosure: Answer composed by an LLM agent. Numbers retrieved by SQL under the user's identity.' Compact chat form: 'Source: Virtue Foundation dataset (facilities sample / NFHS-5). AI-composed.'

props.statement: End every analytical answer with a methodology + AI-disclosure footer (Virtue Foundation dataset source, sample/self-reported caveats, AI-composed); compact one-line form for chat.

nodes.name: no-pie-donut-3d
nodes.content: Chart selection rule: Prefer position/length encodings; never pie/donut/3-D (low-rank angle/area per Cleveland–McGill). Map the user-question class to a compose_infographic archetype.

props.statement: Never pie/donut/3-D — prefer position/length encodings (Cleveland–McGill ranking).

nodes.name: html-side-panel-no-inline-plotly
nodes.content: Direction: the D3 scene engine. The hackathon agent ships rich output as a standalone single-file .html artifact opened in a side panel — never inline Plotly. The chat stays scannable; the visual lives in a dedicated viewport. D3.js (v7+) replaces Plotly for any non-trivial chart.

props.statement: Rich output is a standalone single-file .html artifact in a side panel; D3 (v7+) replaces inline Plotly.

nodes.name: tokens.css
nodes.content:
Single source of truth for the RA-editorial design tokens (CSS vars + mirrored JS + Python): palette (--paper/--oat/--ink/--slate/--mute/--grey/--signal/--amber/--cyan/--magenta/--alarm) and fonts (Source Serif 4, Manrope, JetBrains Mono). The scene engine applies these automatically — colours are NOT passed in. Located at skills/design_system/tokens.css.

Single source of truth for all data-vis colour tokens and their exact hex values (reconciled RA-editorial). Defines --signal #254BB2, --grey #C7C2B6, --amber #DF9B44, --cyan #2695AC, --magenta #913F82, --slate #3A3A40, --mute #9CA0A3, --paper #FAF6EE, --oat #F5EFE3. No colour tokens or hexes may be used outside this file.

Single source of palette + type for the infographic engine: oat/ivory canvas, cobalt #254BB2 primary, amber/cyan/magenta accents, Source Serif 4 display / Manrope / JetBrains Mono. You do not pass colours -- the engine applies them. Referenced as ../tokens.css from the infographics skill.

Canonical design-token source for the RA Signal system. Tokens authored in oklch (signal-*, ink-*, accents, status). The hex values in design.md are the sRGB renderings used by the PPTX renderer. Lives in the compose-pptx skill folder.

props.purpose: Single-source design tokens (palette + fonts) auto-applied by the D3 scene engine

nodes.name: compose_infographic
nodes.content:
The default scene engine. Renders a single-file D3 data story from stored DataFrames: pass an ordered list of typed scene dicts and it renders one chart (1 scene) OR a multi-panel report story (many scenes: kicker -> hero stat cards -> titled chart panels -> methodology). Signature: compose_infographic(title, kicker, lede, stats=[{value,label},...], scenes=[{type, eyebrow, title, lede, variable_name, mapping, data, highlight, caption, ...},...], methodology, source_note). A scene gets data two ways: (1) variable_name + mapping -> the tool shapes the slice from a stored DataFrame (supported for SQL-friendly archetypes: ranked_bar, line_multi, stacked_area, stacked_area_share, lorenz_gini, stat); (2) inline data -> precomputed slice required for statistical archetypes SQL can't produce (forest_ci, bubble_scatter, heatmap_matrix, choropleth, dumbbell, slope, pyramid, bar_race, iceberg, projection, sankey_corridors, kpi_grid) -- compute the dict in run_python_code, read recipes/<type>.md first. Returns {status:'ok', infographic_id, url:'/api/infographics/<id>', scene_count}. Surface ONLY the title in prose -- the frontend auto-opens the side panel from infographic_id; never paste the url or a databricksapps.com/Volumes link. Source: tools/compose_infographic.py.

compose_infographic — the default rich-output tool. 18 native D3 archetypes; one scene = a chart, many scenes = a multi-panel report story. Maps a user-question class to an archetype. Emits a standalone single-file .html artifact. Full catalogue + Cleveland–McGill rationale in infographics/SKILL.md (the PRIMARY composition skill).

The infographic rendering engine. Applies the data-vis colour rules automatically from `tokens.css` — the author does NOT pass colours. Holds the `_SERIES` categorical colour order and honours `scene.highlight` for highlight-by-colour.

Action tool that renders RA-design-system infographics from a scene spec (scenes list, each scene has a type matching a recipe archetype like 'iceberg'). Consumes scene.data inline; for the iceberg recipe the data can be precomputed via run_python_code over a stored DataFrame and passed as the scene's inline data.

Action tool that renders infographic scenes from a spec; each scene names an archetype (e.g. kpi_grid) plus its data. Accepts inline scene.data or a variable_name+mapping shaped by python_shaper into the archetype's data shape. Produces a self-contained infographic; honours the editorial design palette (P.signal cobalt, P.amber, P.alarm) and CSS custom properties.

Tool that renders infographic scenes from a scene spec; the pyramid ChartRecipe is one of its scene types. A scene dict carries type, title, eyebrow, lede, highlight, caption, annotation, mapping, and inline data. python_shaper computes scene data over a stored DataFrame and passes the result as the scene's inline data.

Infographic-composition tool that renders scene-engine recipes (including 'slope') to D3/SVG. Scenes carry inline scene.data (preferred for slope) or optionally a DataFrame-driven python_shaper. Scene data shaping is dispatched in _shape_scene_data() in compose_infographic.py; the slope shaper is not yet wired there (add a t=='slope' branch to enable DataFrame-driven slope), but inline scene.data short-circuits via `scene.get('data') is not None`.

Tool that renders infographic scenes from a recipe archetype + scene.data into an HTML/SVG infographic. forest_ci is one of its scene archetypes: the agent passes scene.data inline (precomputed logistic-regression odds ratios + 95% CIs) and the engine paints a log-axis forest plot with dots, 95% CI whiskers, a dashed OR=1 reference line, and per-row opacity-only motion. Honours the design-system rule 'neutral by default, accent only the named entity' (default rows P.grey, reference row P.ink+bold, scene.highlight P.signal).

Action tool that renders infographic scenes from a scene spec. For the choropleth recipe, the agent passes scene.data inline (shaped via run_python_code over the stored DataFrame using the recipe's python_shaper). Driver/scaffold handles caption/eyebrow/lede; the renderer draws the map paths, labels, legend and serif finding annotation. Loads d3 in its _SCAFFOLD and adds topojson-client as the only extra_cdn for choropleth; fetches the world-atlas atlas at runtime via d3.json.

The action tool that renders a scene-engine infographic scene (one archetype per scene) to an output figure. ChartRecipes such as bar_race are produced by it: the agent passes scene.data (frames + top_n) plus scene meta (highlight, value_label, annotations, top_n) and the renderer paints the figure (latest frame first as resting geometry, then optional earliest→latest replay loop). Renders static-or-autoplay figures, not controllable widgets.

Action tool the orchestrator calls to render a registered infographic scene (e.g. sankey_corridors) into a self-contained HTML chart. The agent first runs the recipe's python_shaper in run_python_code over the stored DataFrame to produce scene.data, then passes it as the scene's inline data. To register a new archetype: add its name to the Python _ARCHETYPES set and to the tool's scenes type-enum docstring so the orchestrator can route to it. Scenes consume scene.title, scene.highlight, scene.top_n.

Action tool that renders an infographic from a scene spec. Consumes a ChartRecipe archetype (e.g. bubble_scatter) plus scene.data and produces the rendered infographic. For bubble_scatter the agent supplies precomputed log-log OLS stats (intercept/slope/r/r2/p) inline via scene["data"]; an optional run_python_code python_shaper assists when a DataFrame + column mapping are supplied.

Tool the agent calls to PRODUCE infographic scenes from a recipe + scene.data. The dumbbell recipe is one of its scene archetypes. Either pass the shaped dict inline as the scene's `data`, or precompute via the recipe's python shaper. CSS uses only existing engine classes (axis-title, vlabel, clabel, annot) with no injected <style>; axis lines use P.hair to match the engine's .axis stroke. Colours come from the engine palette P (P.grey default, P.amber up-mover, P.cyan down-mover, P.signal highlight).

Action tool that renders an infographic scene from a recipe + scene.data into an HTML/SVG figure. For heatmap_matrix it consumes scene.data {origins, dests, cells, cell_min, spread} (or computes it via python_shaper / run_python_code over a stored DataFrame), draws the cobalt matrix (viewBox 1040x700, .figure svg width:100% height:auto), prints each cell % (paper text when v>0.45 on deep cobalt fill, else ink), hatches suppressed/omitted cells via SVG pattern url(#hm-hatch), and boxes+annotates the highlighted origin row ('N-pt swing ->'). Motion: final geometry set at t=0, H.in animates opacity only (reduced-motion safe, headless-Chrome screenshot at t=0 shows the complete matrix).

Action tool that renders an infographic from a scene spec (scene.type + scene.data). Iterates scenes and dispatches each to its recipe renderer (here: 'projection'). Uses inline scene data verbatim via _shape_scene_data; a python_shaper helper covers the natural DataFrame->shape case (long table with x_col + y_col + optional series_col/role_col/lo_col/hi_col). For OLS/CAGR-derived projection points and crossing years (not computable in SQL), the agent passes the scene data inline. DATA-SHAPE CONTRACTS (added 2026-06-11): heatmap_matrix is ONLY for an origin-by-destination rate matrix ({origins:[{iso,name}],dests:[{iso,name}],cells:[{o,d,v}]}); age-sex / demographic breakdowns use the pyramid archetype ({bands:[{age,female,male}]} or long-format {rows:[{age_band,sex,value}]}), never heatmap_matrix. A stat scene's data must carry a numeric value (or value_fmt); bubble_scatter points are {name,gdp,per1000,hosted,region}. If the data does not fit an archetype's contract, choose a simpler archetype (ranked_bar / line_multi) instead of forcing it.

RENDERER GUARANTEES (added 2026-06-12): line_multi ZOOMS the y-axis to the data when the series sit far above zero (min > 25% of max), prints an explicit 'Y-AXIS ZOOMED -- STARTS AT X, NOT ZERO' axis note (pass data.y0=0 to force a zero baseline), DODGES converging end-of-line labels (>=15px gaps + leader lines, right margin 150) and with >8 series labels only the important ones (highlighted + extremes + biggest movers) -- do NOT pre-trim series just to avoid label overlap. heatmap_matrix editorially shortens + truncates long axis names (full name kept as an svg <title> tooltip) and computes its top margin from the rotated header extent so angled headers always clear the canvas.

props.produces: standalone single-file .html infographic / multi-panel report story

nodes.name: compose_story
nodes.content:
Freehand flagship escape hatch -- use ONLY when the user wants a bespoke scroll-driven essay (e.g. a 3-chapter sticky-scroll narrative where the chart in a pinned panel SWAPS as the reader scrolls past prose steps) that the scene archetypes can't express. Otherwise use compose_infographic. Signature: compose_story(title, template_html, data). It does NOT execute your code -- it injects your data dict at the '__DATA__' token (and the brand palette at an optional '__PALETTE__' token), then publishes. Workflow: (1) start from recipes/_flagship_scaffold.html (RA-branded scrollytelling skeleton), (2) compute the data dict in run_python_code over stored DataFrames, (3) pass template_html + data. No number may be hand-typed in the template. THE MOTION IS THE SCROLL (non-negotiable): the sticky chart is a state machine, not a one-shot drawing -- each chapter's renderer render(svg, ch, ci, si) draws the chart in the state for step si, called once on load (si=0) and again on every step change, interpolating with a d3 transition. Step 0 must render the complete chart; every per-step change goes through T() (a real transition live, no-op under prefers-reduced-motion) so PNG/PDF exports stay static. Source: tools/compose_story.py.

compose_story — freehand bespoke scrollytelling escape hatch (vs compose_infographic's archetype catalogue). Emits a standalone single-file .html scrolly. HARD RULES (added 2026-06-11 after live bugs): (1) RENDER-GUARD — track the active step per chapter (lastSi[]) and re-render ONLY when it changes; never call a renderer unconditionally in a scroll handler (the page strobes and replays entrance animations). (2) SCENE-SWAP CLEANUP — when steps switch between different chart layouts in one sticky svg, each layout draws in its OWN root group (g.scene-<name>) and every scene fn first removes all OTHER scenes' roots; selector-list cleanup (selectAll('rect.bar,text.label')) misses elements and leaves scene A's bars overlapping scene B when the reader scrolls back up. (3) The sticky panel background must be OPAQUE (translucent rgba lets the methodology section bleed through it). (4) Clamp value labels: when a bar exceeds ~60% of the inner width, anchor the label INSIDE the bar end (text-anchor end, white fill) so long text never overflows the panel. (5) PANEL FITS ITS GRID COLUMN: width:100% (+ max-width), never a fixed px width wider than the column — an oversized panel overlaps and covers the step cards (live bug 2026-06-11). (6) Chart titles/subtitles anchor at the svg left edge (x~24, not the plot margin) and stay short — clipped titles are a recurring live bug. (7) AXIS TEXT STYLING (live bug 2026-06-12) -- style EVERY svg text node with an explicit font-size: a node classed 'axis dest' does NOT match a '.axis text' descendant CSS rule and silently renders at the 16px body font, breaking all label-collision math. (8) ROTATED HEADER BAND -- heatmap column headers sit in their OWN reserved band BELOW the title/subtitle: grid top margin >= subtitle bottom + sin(angle)*longest-label-width + 10px; headers must never cross the title; shorten long names (United Kingdom->UK, Switzerland->Switz., Luxembourg->Lux., Netherlands->Neth.). (9) LINE-CHART Y-DOMAIN (live bug 2026-06-12) -- never hard-code [0,100]: fit the domain to the data (nice floor below the min; lines may zoom, bars may never) and declare it in the subtitle ('y-axis zoomed: starts at 20%, not zero'); a forced 0-100 domain crushes converging rate series into an unreadable band. (10) END-LABEL DODGING -- end-of-line labels ('Name 99.4%') need a right margin sized for the longest label (~7px/char at 12px bold) AND vertical dodging when series converge: sort by y, enforce >=15px gaps, clamp into the plot, draw a 1px leader line to any label that moved. (11) LABEL BUDGET -- with >8 series label only the IMPORTANT ones (highlighted + final-value extremes + biggest movers); unlabelled series stay thin grey context lines.

[editorial 2026-06-12] STORY ARC = martini glass: an establishing hook chart that lands in <3s, then 3-5 focused beats (one question each), then the takeaway. Keep axes FROZEN across step transitions (no domain re-scaling between steps of the same scene); captions always visible — never hover-only; entrance reveals play ONCE (steps already seen never re-animate).

nodes.name: stat
nodes.content:
Archetype for 'One headline number with weight' (or count_up variant). Encoding: text. Data via variable_name + mapping (SQL-friendly). Use for a single number rather than a whole chart. Recipe at the find_skill graph.

stat — single big stat value with weight, for a single KPI question ("how many districts have zero facilities?") when plain text needs more emphasis.

props.when_to_use: Single KPI that needs visual weight beyond plain text

nodes.name: kpi_grid
nodes.content:
Infographic recipe rendering a grid of KPI stat cards (one card per metric/row). Data shape (scene.data): {cards:[{value:number|string, value_fmt?:string (pre-formatted display e.g. "6.1%", "29 in 30", "1.89M" — overrides value), label:string (also matched against scene.highlight), delta?:number|string (signed change e.g. "+2.1M"/"-14%"/0.12), delta_dir?:"up"|"down" (inferred from delta sign if omitted), sub?:string (dim one-line context)}], columns?:number (fixed column-count hint, else auto-fit)}. When to use: hero reframes and hand-curated KPI sets — a flat grid of big numbers with optional delta arrows and context. value_fmt is the escape hatch for percentages, ratios, and "N in M" framings H.fmt cannot express; numeric value runs through H.fmt (K/M/B). scene.highlight (string or array of label strings) recolours that card's value to its accent; all others stay cobalt (P.signal). Delta colours: up→P.amber, down→P.alarm. scene.annotations[0], if present, renders as an italic-serif finding under the grid. Compute: shape one card per DataFrame row in run_python_code (mapping = {value_col, label_col, value_fmt_col?, delta_col?, delta_dir_col?, sub_col?, top_n?}; defaults: value_col=first numeric col, label_col=first non-numeric col, top_n=6) then pass as scene's inline data; python_shaper handles the natural DataFrame→grid path; agents can also pass data.cards inline. HTML-not-SVG by design (does NOT call H.svg) — appends HTML divs for crisp text, .figure.bare CSS strips card chrome so the grid sits flat matching the stat renderer. Motion is screenshot-safe: full text painted immediately, then H.in fades opacity only (staggered); final values present at t=0 under reduced-motion/backgrounded tabs, no width/transform reveals. Draws all colours/fonts from CSS custom properties (var(--serif/sans/mono)) and the P palette — zero new colours, no injected <style>. Delta arrows use Unicode geometric triangles (▲/▼), not emoji. Register kpi_grid shaping in _shape_scene_data if wiring the DataFrame path (inline-data path already works via scene.data passthrough).

Archetype for 'A few headline numbers + deltas'. Encoding: text + length(delta). Inline data required (compute in run_python_code). Use instead of a pie/donut for a small set of headline figures. Recipe at recipes/kpi_grid.md.

Grid of headline KPI numbers — the preferred alternative to pie/donut when summarizing a few key magnitudes.

kpi_grid — grid of a few headline numbers plus deltas.

props.when_to_use: A few headline numbers + deltas

nodes.name: ranked_bar
nodes.content:
Archetype for the question 'Who's biggest? rank / leaderboard'. Encoding: length on a common baseline (highest Cleveland-McGill rank for this question). Data via variable_name + mapping (SQL-friendly): mapping={label_col, value_col}; supports highlight, value_label, top_n. Prefer over a donut for composition. When a Top-N leaderboard, filter sentinel rows: coo_iso NOT IN ('UNK','Various','-',...). Recipe at recipes/ranked_bar.md.

Ranked bar chart — the preferred alternative to pie/donut for part-to-whole or magnitude comparison (bars use position/length, a high-rank encoding).

ranked_bar — Top-N leaderboard; highlight the named entity (in --signal cobalt, others --grey).

props.data_shape: categorical N with one value each
props.when_to_use: Top-N leaderboard; highlight the named entity

nodes.name: line_multi
nodes.content:
Archetype for 'How has X changed over time?'. Encoding: position. Data via variable_name + mapping (SQL-friendly). Recipe at recipes/line_multi.md.

line_multi — multi-series time series / comparison over time.

props.when_to_use: Time series / comparison

nodes.name: stacked_area
nodes.content:
Archetype for 'What's the absolute composition over time?'. Encoding: position/length. Data via variable_name + mapping (SQL-friendly). Recipe at recipes/stacked_area.md.

stacked_area / stacked_area_share — composition over time, absolute (stacked_area) or share (stacked_area_share).

[editorial 2026-06-12] Max 3 bands — only the BOTTOM band reads accurately off the axis; more bands → switch to lines (line_multi) or small multiples. Small multiples share ONE axis scale across panels.

props.when_to_use: Composition over time (absolute / share)

nodes.name: lorenz_gini
nodes.content:
Archetype for 'How concentrated / unequal is it?'. Encoding: position + area (the diagonal is perfect equality; the bowed line is reality). Data via variable_name + mapping (SQL-friendly), e.g. variable_name='district_facility_counts', mapping={value_col:'facilities'}. Gini computed across all districts with >=1 facility. Recipe at the find_skill graph.

lorenz_gini — concentration / inequality (Lorenz curve + Gini coefficient), e.g. facility coverage across districts.

props.when_to_use: Concentration / inequality

nodes.name: bubble_scatter
nodes.content:
Infographic recipe: a log-log bubble scatter, one bubble per district. X = an urbanisation proxy (log, >0); Y = facility count or an access metric (log, >0); bubble AREA = sample size / survey women; COLOUR ENCODES REGION (d3.scaleOrdinal over the `regions` series, NOT highlight-by-colour). Used to test whether facility supply tracks need — and to show the honest null once urbanisation is controlled. Data shape (scene.data): points:[{name, region, gdp, per1000, hosted, note}], regions:[...] (legend+colour domain; if omitted, derived first-seen from points), and PRECOMPUTED log-log OLS stats intercept/slope/r/r2/p (all optional; omit intercept+slope -> no fit line), plus optional x_label/y_label axis overrides. note:true labels a point (notable outlier); scene.highlight (string|array of point names) ADDS labels on top of note:true points. Points with non-positive x/y/size are dropped (log scale). KEY: r/r2/p/slope/intercept are PRECOMPUTED by the agent (log-log OLS — SQL/pandas alone can't do it), passed inline via scene["data"]; the python_shaper only assists when a DataFrame + mapping is also supplied (shapes the points array, preserves the agent's stat keys), else returns scene["data"] verbatim. Field names gdp/per1000/hosted are the generic data-shape contract keys (kept stable) — map your urban-proxy/facility/sample columns onto them. Motion: every element draws at FINAL geometry (cx/cy/r/line endpoints immediate), only opacity fades via H.in; bubble fill uses fill-opacity ATTR (not style opacity); fade delay capped Math.min(i*18,700). r/R² annotation anchored top-left (x=8,y=14) so it never collides with the regression line or right-gutter legends. Bubble stroke = P.paper (canvas) for halo separation. Pure d3 v7, no CDN.

Archetype for 'Does need predict supply? (correlation, honest null)'. Encoding: position (log-log) + area. Statistical archetype -- inline data REQUIRED: OLS fit computed in run_python_code. The raw supply-vs-need correlation is CONFOUNDED by urbanisation (R-confounder-urbanisation) — report the controlled coefficient. Recipe at the find_skill graph.

bubble_scatter — correlation / honest null; log–log axes + OLS fit.

props.when_to_use: Correlation / honest null (log–log + OLS)

nodes.name: choropleth
nodes.content:
Choropleth map recipe for compose_infographic. ARCHETYPE: filled-region map shaded by a sequential cobalt ramp, used for any 0..1 metric BY GEOGRAPHY — e.g. 'facility coverage or an NFHS-5 indicator by district/state'. DATA SHAPE (scene.data): d = { vals: { <numericISO(int)>: { name: string, rate: number(0..1), n?: number } }, label_iso?: number[], frame?: [[lon,lat]x4], finding?: string, atlas_url?: string }. vals is REQUIRED and MUST be keyed by the geography's numeric feature id because the TopoJSON keys features by feature.id (numeric code). label_iso = optional list of numeric ids to direct-label; if omitted/empty, ALL valued regions are labelled. frame = optional 4 corner points for the map MultiPoint frame; set it to the India bounding box (roughly [[68,6],[97,6],[97,37],[68,37]]) to frame India. finding = optional inline serif annotation, only used if scene.annotations[0] is absent. atlas_url = optional override for the TopoJSON URL (point it at an India districts/states topojson). SCENE META consumed: scene.title (aria), scene.value_label (legend heading, e.g. 'facility coverage'), scene.highlight (string name OR array of names -> accented signal-coloured label + ink-stroke outline; matched against vals[*].name NOT id), scene.annotations[0] (preferred serif finding text). KEY SNIPPET — minimal data: {"vals":{"<id>":{"name":"Araria","rate":0.0,"n":120}, ...},"finding":"Araria records among the highest health-burden indices with no facilities in this ~10k sample."}. WHEN TO USE: pick this for a single metric mapped over geography keyed by a numeric feature id — ideal for coverage-by-district / indicator-by-state. VALIDATED in headless Chrome on a real TopoJSON atlas (country paths, cobalt-ramp fills, direct labels w/ paper halo, ink-stroke highlight outline, sequential legend + no-data swatch, serif annotation — zero JS console errors). MOTION: fully screenshot-safe — all final geometry drawn with real attrs first; H.in only fades the <g> opacity 0->1 (no width/r/dashoffset reveal); correct geometry at t=0. extra_cdn: ONLY topojson-client added (d3 already in _SCAFFOLD); the atlas itself is fetched at runtime via d3.json from CDN, NOT a <script>, so NOT in extra_cdn. Graceful fallback: if the CDN fetch fails or topojson is missing, the renderer paints a red annot message instead of throwing. Each render uses a random uid for its <linearGradient> so multiple choropleth scenes on one page don't collide.

Map recipe that encodes an ordered numeric measure per geography via colour lightness. Styled by the sequential cobalt ramp (#EAF0FB→#254BB2).

choropleth — geographic pattern, India-framed.

Archetype for 'Where, geographically?'. Encoding: shading (India-framed). Inline data REQUIRED. Recipe at the find_skill graph.

[editorial] Choropleths map RATES (coverage, share, 0..1 metrics), never raw totals — area size distorts totals. Render no-data districts DISTINCTLY from zero (light grey hatch/neutral, excluded from the ramp), never as the lowest ramp colour.

props.when_to_use: Geographic pattern (India-framed)

nodes.name: dumbbell
nodes.content:
Dumbbell / slope chart between TWO ranked metric columns that share a join key (rank 1 = top). Despite the filename, it renders a SLOPE chart: two vertical axes (xL=370/xR=560), rows placed by rank (rowH=40, TOP=120), rank ticks in the inner gutter, names+values OUTSIDE each axis, italic-serif standout annotations 15px above the named row. Connectors are drawn ONLY for the shared 'movers' (entities on BOTH columns), behind the dots; up=rises on metric B, down=falls. Big dots (r6) for movers+standouts, small (r4.5) otherwise. VIEWBOX 920 wide, height auto-grows (TOP + n*rowH + BOT), ~12 rows/column.

DATA SHAPE (scene.data):
d = {
  left:  [ {key, name, rank, value, value_fmt?, standout?} ... ],   // metric-A column, rank 1 = top
  right: [ {key, name, rank, value, value_fmt?, standout?} ... ],   // metric-B column
  movers:[ {key, dir} ... ],   // on BOTH columns; dir 'up' (amber) | 'down' (cyan)
  left_head, right_head: column headers (uppercased)
  left_sub?, right_sub?: optional sub-headers
  annotations?: [ {side:'left'|'right', rank, text} ... ]
}
`key` joins left<->right<->movers (e.g. district name). `value` numeric (formatted via H.fmt unless value_fmt given). `standout:true` accents a row on only ONE column (e.g. a district ranked high on raw need but low once urbanisation-adjusted). scene.highlight (name or key, string or array) overrides to accent any entity with P.signal; otherwise movers=amber(up)/cyan(down), everything else grey. movers MUST list only entities on BOTH columns (renderer filter-guards against stray connectors).

WHEN TO USE: comparing the same set of entities across two different ranked metrics/units (e.g. raw health-burden rank vs urbanisation-adjusted rank) to show who rises/falls between the two rankings.

Archetype for 'Two-state comparison / gap per row'. Encoding: position + length(gap). Inline data REQUIRED. Prefer over two separate bars for a 2-state gap. Part of the reframe spine (e.g. supply-vs-need Simpson's paradox once you adjust for urbanisation). Recipe at the find_skill graph.

dumbbell / slope — two-state gap or rank reversal between two points.

props.when_to_use: Two-state gap / rank reversal

nodes.name: pyramid
nodes.content:
Population pyramid (two-sided diverging bar) infographic recipe. DATA SHAPE (scene.data): {bands: [{age: string, female: number, male: number}, ...], unit?: string}. Rows are passed top->bottom in display order; the renderer preserves array order. female=left / male=right diverging bars. unit (e.g. 'M' for millions) is appended to axis ticks; '' for raw counts. WHEN TO USE: any two-sided diverging-bar breakdown across an ordered band axis (the field names female/male and 'age' are the generic data-shape contract — reuse them for any two-category split across ordered bands). KEY ENCODING: two-tone left=signal (cobalt primary, P.signal) / right=slate (P.slate) — this split IS the chart's meaning and is NOT collapsed to a single highlight colour. scene.highlight: 'female' dims the right side (foreground the left category, fill-opacity 0.30); 'male' dims the left side; null/absent = both sides full strength (fill-opacity 0.92). scene.annotation (optional): string OR {text, side?:'left'|'right', band_index?:int} — an italic-serif finding annotation; default left of top band; also reads scene.annotations[0]. RENDER: symmetric linear x-scale domain [-maxV, maxV] with maxV = 1.12 * max(any cell), centred band labels, side headers above the axes, abs-value axis ticks with unit suffix. Axis tick formatter: integers/commas for >=1e3 via H.fmt, else 1-dp for fractional millions, then unit suffix. viewBox 720x460 (wide; slightly taller than 420 default to give 5-7 bands room — adjust via Hh for many bands). MOTION: final geometry set immediately on every rect; H.in animates only element opacity; dim uses fill-opacity ATTR so it survives — element-opacity fade composes with fill-opacity dim without clobbering; a backgrounded/headless screenshot at t=0 shows full correct geometry. Uses ONLY existing CSS classes (axis, clabel, annot) + palette/H helpers; no injected <style>, no extra CDN. Inline scene.data takes precedence (returned verbatim) so the agent can hand-build the breakdown when the split is computed outside SQL.

Archetype for 'A two-sided diverging breakdown across ordered bands'. Encoding: length, diverging from centre. Inline data REQUIRED. Recipe at the find_skill graph.

pyramid — two-sided diverging-bar structure across ordered bands.

props.when_to_use: Two-sided diverging breakdown across ordered bands

nodes.name: bar_race
nodes.content:
Animated ranking-over-time infographic (a bar chart race). Data shape (scene.data): d = {frames:[{year:<number|string>, rows:[{label:<str>, value:<number>, color?:<hex>}]}, ...], top_n?:<number> (bars per frame; default 10 or scene.top_n)}. One frame per time step ordered earliest→latest; each frame's rows is the FULL field for that step (any length) — the renderer filters value>0, sorts desc, slices to top_n, and re-ranks per frame so bars reorder. color is optional per-row; if omitted bars render neutral P.grey and only the entity named in scene.highlight is accented via H.hue. Scene meta used: scene.highlight (label to accent), scene.value_label (axis-title text), scene.annotations[0] (one italic-serif finding annotation), scene.top_n (fallback for d.top_n). The LATEST frame is rendered first as the resting state; the loop (if motion allowed) replays earliest→latest and ends back on the latest. When to use: showing how a ranking of entities changes across ordered time steps. Faithful port of the reference race renderer adapted to the scene-engine contract; dropped the interactive play/pause/scrub/speed UI and linear inter-step interpolation — frames are discrete with a fixed hold. python_shaper expects a long/tidy DataFrame (one row per step×entity) with mapping {year_col, label_col, value_col, color_col?, top_n?}; auto-detects (year = numeric col in 1900–2100; value = first numeric; label = first remaining). For inline data pass d directly on the scene.

Archetype for 'Who led over time (changing ranks)'. Encoding: length + motion. Inline data REQUIRED. Recipe at the find_skill graph.

bar_race — changing ranks over time (animated).

props.when_to_use: Changing ranks over time

nodes.name: iceberg
nodes.content:
Iceberg infographic recipe (RA infographics design system). DATA SHAPE (scene.data): d = {above: {label: string, value: number}, below: {label: string, value: number}} where 'above' = the visible/above-waterline segment (e.g. 'Districts with at least one recorded facility') and 'below' = the hidden/below-waterline mass (e.g. 'Zero-facility districts in the ~10k sample'). Values are raw counts in the SAME unit; renderer only uses ratios value/(above+below) for the bar split + % labels, so absolute scale is arbitrary as long as both share it. The bar is always 100% wide: above starts at x=0, below stacks immediately after. scene.highlight (optional) = label of the segment to accent in signal-cobalt (matched against above.label/below.label); if omitted, above renders signal cobalt and below renders neutral grey. scene.value_label (optional) = a small uppercase footer unit string. WHEN TO USE: to reframe a single quantity as a visible tip vs a hidden mass — collapse a population into a 'what the headline counts' (above) vs 'the larger hidden group' (below) contrast (canonical: 'the facility sample shows coverage, but most districts have no recorded facility'). VISUAL: single 100%-width horizontal bar, white % labels inside each segment, dashed waterline rule on the boundary, an 'X% have no recorded facility' serif annotation under the below-mass, and a tip annotation above the line. viewBox 720x420 (wide chart, consistent with line_multi/stacked_area). Uses only existing CSS classes (vlabel, annot, axis-title) and only P palette colours + '#fff' for in-bar labels; no <style> injected, no extra CDN. Simplified to the 2-segment {above,below} contract (same reframe, same waterline position).

Archetype for 'The reframe: the number behind the number'. Encoding: length, above/below a waterline. Inline data REQUIRED. Canonical reframe: a coverage headline -> most districts have no recorded facility in the sample. Recipe at the find_skill graph.

iceberg — the reframe; the number behind the number.

props.when_to_use: The reframe (number behind the number)

nodes.name: projection
nodes.content:
Infographic recipe for a trend projection/forecast line chart: solid observed HISTORY line(s) + n dashed PROJECTION lines (e.g. a linear OLS fit and a compounding exponential), with an optional shaded uncertainty FAN (band lo/hi) between the paths over the projection horizon. Data shape (scene.data): series:[{name, role:'history'|'projection', dashed?:bool, points:[{x:number,y:number}]}] (render order = SERIES colour order; 1..n named lines); band:[{x,lo,hi}] optional uncertainty fan; split_year:number optional vertical rule (solid history ends / dashed projection begins); threshold:number optional horizontal reference line (e.g. 150M) with threshold_label (defaults '<fmt(threshold)> threshold'); markers:[{x,label}] optional dots on the threshold line (crossing years); crisis:[{x,label,y?}] event annotations dropped on the history line (y auto-snaps to nearest history point if omitted); fit_lo/fit_hi:number optional -> faint amber shade + 'fitted on YYYY-YYYY' caption (fit_label override); y0 optional y-axis floor (default 0); y_format:'num'|'pct' (default 'num' = H.fmt K/M/B; 'pct' = 0-dp percent); y_suffix:'M' optional unit appended to numeric y ticks/threshold label. Scene-level: highlight (string|array of series/marker names -> accents that path/marker via H.hue, grey otherwise); value_label (y-axis title). WHEN TO USE: 'if the trend just continues' extrapolations / forecasts of a time series, especially when contrasting two naive projection paths (linear vs exponential) and showing the gap between them as uncertainty. Data shape = a long table with x_col + y_col (+ optional series_col / role_col / lo_col / hi_col); OLS/CAGR-derived projection points and crossing years can't be computed in SQL so the agent passes the scene data inline (tool uses inline data verbatim per _shape_scene_data). MOTION-compliant: every line drawn with its FINAL d immediately (dashed projections get resting stroke-dasharray '6,5', band gets resting fill-opacity .22), H.in fades OPACITY only — no draw-on, no clip-rect growth, no width/r reveals; t=0 screenshot / backgrounded tab shows correct geometry; reduced-motion-safe via H.in. Colour: history = dark ink (the spine); projection lines + markers/crossing-dots take SERIES[] palette colours. Uses ONLY existing CSS classes (axis, axis-title, clabel, annot) + P palette + SERIES; no injected <style>, no extra CDN. viewBox 720x440 matches the wide line_multi sibling. Faithful port of build_nextdecade.py adapted to the scene-engine contract; a single-line projection works (one series, role 'history').

Archetype for 'Where is this heading?'. Encoding: position + uncertainty band. Inline data REQUIRED. Recipe at recipes/projection.md.

projection — where it's heading (forward projection).

props.when_to_use: Where it's heading

nodes.name: slope
nodes.content:
Slope (slopegraph) infographic recipe. Data-shape: scene.data = { left_label (string, x-position label for first column e.g. 'Raw rate'), right_label (string, x-position label for second column e.g. 'Adjusted'), y_format ('pct'|'num', optional default 'pct'; 'pct' expects values in 0..1), y0 (number, optional y-axis floor default 0), note (string, optional bottom-left italic finding annotation about the crossing), rows: [{ name (entity name, matched against scene.highlight), left (number, value at left x-position), right (number, value at right x-position), or (number, optional left-column rank for '#or -> #sr' annotation on highlighted rows), sr (number, optional right-column rank), note (string, optional per-row annotation used if or/sr absent) }] }. scene.highlight = a name string OR array of names; only highlighted entities get an accent colour, value labels, name label, and the rank-reversal annotation; every other line renders neutral grey. scene.title used as svg aria-label. When-to-use: showing rank reversal / Simpson's paradox between two measures of the same entities — crossing lines are the whole point (e.g. raw supply-vs-need rank vs urbanisation-adjusted rank, where the ranking 'lied'). Render: two x-positions via d3.scalePoint, one line per entity connecting left->right, endpoint circles at both ends, percent y-axis, italic-serif rank-reversal annotation ('#or -> #sr') and an entity name label on the highlighted line(s). MOTION: fully compliant — all final geometry drawn immediately at correct coordinates; H.in animates OPACITY only; partial transparency on neutral lines uses the stroke-opacity ATTR (0.7), not style opacity, so H.in's tween never clobbers it; correct at t=0 under headless-Chrome rasterization and reduced-motion. CSS: uses only existing classes (axis, vlabel, clabel, annot) and CSS vars/P hexes, no injected <style>; value y-axis assumed 0..1 fractions when y_format='pct', pass y_format:'num' + a value_label scene field for absolute numbers. The python_shaper is OPTIONAL and additive — slope rows are usually best passed inline as scene.data (the reference precomputes overall/adjusted rates + ranks that SQL alone can't produce). Shaper is NOT yet wired into _shape_scene_data() in compose_infographic.py — to enable DataFrame-driven slope scenes, add a t=='slope' branch there calling the shaper body; inline scene.data already works via the existing `scene.get('data') is not None` short-circuit. Shaper mapping keys: name_col, left_col, right_col, optional left_label/right_label, rank_left_col, rank_right_col, y_format.

Archetype for 'Rank reversal / Simpson's paradox'. Encoding: position + crossing lines. Inline data REQUIRED. Part of the reframe spine (supply-vs-need -> Simpson's paradox once adjusted for urbanisation). Recipe at the find_skill graph.

slope — two-state gap / rank reversal as a slope chart (paired with dumbbell).

props.when_to_use: Two-state gap / rank reversal

nodes.name: stacked_area_share
nodes.content:
Archetype for 'How has the share mix shifted?' (100% stacked). Encoding: position. Data via variable_name + mapping (SQL-friendly). Recipe at recipes/stacked_area_share.md.

stacked_area_share — composition over time as a share (100%) stack.

[editorial 2026-06-12] Max 3 bands — only the bottom band reads accurately; beyond that use line_multi or small multiples (which share one axis scale).

props.when_to_use: Composition over time (share)

nodes.name: infographics/SKILL.md
nodes.content: PRIMARY composition skill for the hackathon design system. Archetype catalogue + when-to-pick + data-injection model + report-story structure + voice + recipes + Cleveland–McGill rationale. Read before calling compose_infographic. This SKILL.md pins brand + voice; infographics/SKILL.md tells you how to compose.

props.purpose: Primary composition skill: archetype catalogue + decision tree for compose_infographic

nodes.name: data-visualisation-chart-guidance/SKILL.md
nodes.content: General chart-selection matrix + design rules (legacy from v3). Useful for cross-referencing chart-type choice but supplanted by the infographics decision tree for the hackathon agent.

props.purpose: Legacy general chart-selection matrix + design rules (cross-reference only)

nodes.name: chart_guidance_seven_chart_set
nodes.content: The Data Visualisation Chart Guidance skill is intentionally limited to seven chart types only: Bar, Line, Area, Pie, Donut, Bubble, Radar. Do not recommend any chart outside this list. If none of the seven fits the request, say so explicitly and explain why none fits and what data relationship is missing from the supported set.

props.statement: Restrict all chart recommendations to the seven supported types (Bar, Line, Area, Pie, Donut, Bubble, Radar); reject explicitly if none fits.

nodes.name: chart_operating_principle
nodes.content: Operating Principle: Prioritise communication accuracy over visual novelty. Choose the chart that makes the intended message easiest to read and hardest to misinterpret. Do not force a familiar chart type when the data relationship does not support it.

props.statement: Prioritise communication accuracy over visual novelty; do not force a familiar chart when the data relationship does not support it.

nodes.name: chart_response_contract
nodes.content: Response Contract — structure a chart recommendation in this order: (1) State the data relationship being shown. (2) Recommend one chart type from the allowed seven, or explicitly reject all seven. (3) Explain why the recommendation fits. (4) Explain why the nearest alternative chart types are weaker. (5) Provide a visual spec for the chosen chart. (6) Provide approved colour tokens and hex values. (7) List warnings and anti-patterns for the chosen chart. If none fit, answer: no supported chart is appropriate, why none of the seven fits, and what data relationship is missing from the supported set.

props.statement: Answer in order: relationship → recommend/reject → why fits → why alternatives weaker → visual spec → colour tokens+hex → warnings/anti-patterns.

nodes.name: chart_selection_checks
nodes.content: Selection Checks — before choosing a chart, confirm: Is the message comparison, trend, composition, correlation, or profile? Is the x-axis ordered or unordered? How many categories or series are present? Does the audience need precise comparison or only pattern recognition? Does the chart need ranking, direct labels, or a center total? Reference the chart-selection matrix, detailed chart rules, and colour/token rules in the bundled references.

props.statement: Before picking a chart, classify the message (comparison/trend/composition/correlation/profile), x-axis order, category/series count, precision need, and ranking/labels/center-total needs.

nodes.name: chart_required_behaviour
nodes.content: Required Behaviour: Stay light-theme only for v1. Use only the documented data-visualisation tokens and hex values from the bundled references. Keep recommendations within the supported seven-chart set. Use semantic colours only when the chart is explicitly encoding status semantics. Keep visual specifications concrete: axes, labels, legends, fills, ordering, and colour assignment. Reject misleading chart choices instead of softening the answer.

props.statement: Light-theme v1 only; documented tokens/hex only; semantic colours only for status encoding; concrete specs; reject misleading choices rather than soften.

nodes.name: chart_anti_patterns
nodes.content: Anti-patterns a weak answer commits: names a chart without explaining the relationship; uses pie or donut for too many slices; uses line for unordered categories; uses area when the task needs precise multi-series comparison; uses bubble when x, y, and size are not all quantitative; uses radar for exact reading tasks. A strong answer names the actual data relationship first, chooses for readability/interpretability, explains why nearby alternatives are weaker, and references the design system's tokens and documented hex values when colour matters.

props.statement: Avoid: chart without relationship; pie/donut with too many slices; line on unordered categories; area for precise multi-series; bubble when x/y/size not all quantitative; radar for exact reads.

nodes.name: chart_pull_specific_rules
nodes.content: Chart-Specific Detail: For the selected chart, always pull the detailed rules from references/chart-guidelines.md, including When to use, When not to use, Required data shape, Visual structure rules, Label and legend rules, Colour rules, and Anti-patterns. Do not answer with only generic advice when the chart-specific reference provides a clearer rule.

props.statement: For the chosen chart, always pull the detailed per-chart rules from chart-guidelines.md rather than giving generic advice.

nodes.name: chart_long_vs_wide_format
nodes.content: Data Shape — Long vs Wide format: Before calling render_chart, inspect the DataFrame shape via describe_dataframe. WIDE format (one column per series, e.g. Division | Jan | Feb | Mar | Dec) → render_chart(chart_type='line', x='Division', y='Jan,Feb,Mar,Dec'); each y column becomes a trace. LONG format (one row per (category, x) pair, e.g. Division | Month | Activity_Ratio) → render_chart(chart_type='line', x='Month', y='Activity_Ratio', color_col='Division'); each unique color_col value becomes a trace. NEVER render a line/bar/area on long-format data without color_col — the tool collapses all rows into a single trace and the chart zigzags through every point in row order; the validator catches this and returns a validation_error with the fix. If color_col isn't obvious (mixed long/wide, >1 candidate categorical column), pivot to wide first via query_stored_dfs (SELECT Division, MAX(CASE WHEN Month=1 THEN Activity_Ratio END) AS Jan, ... GROUP BY Division) then chart the pivoted result.

props.statement: Inspect shape via describe_dataframe first; wide format → comma-list y columns; long format → set color_col; never line/bar/area on long-format without color_col (validator rejects); pivot to wide via query_stored_dfs when color_col ambiguous.

nodes.name: bar
nodes.content:
Bar chart — one of the seven supported chart types. Use for comparison across categories, especially when precise comparison or ranking is needed. Inspect data shape via describe_dataframe before rendering: wide format → comma-list each series column in y; long format → set color_col so each category becomes its own trace.

Bar chart. Use when users need to compare lengths, ranking, or category differences across discrete categories or simple buckets. Choose Bar for ranking, side-by-side category comparison, or simple bucketed comparison. Avoid when the main message is continuous change over an ordered axis.

Bar chart. When to use: compare discrete categories; show ranking highest-to-lowest or lowest-to-highest; compare simple buckets when continuity between points is not the message. When NOT to use: the message is continuous change over an ordered axis; too many categories for readable labels; bars become too thin to compare confidently. Required data shape: one quantitative measure per category (or a small number of grouped/stacked measures); categorical labels on one axis; a zero baseline for bar-length comparison.

Horizontal bars. Use for ranking many items where N>7 (easier to label than vertical column). Native editable PowerPoint chart.

Bar chart — use for ranked categorical comparisons, e.g. top-10 districts by facility count. Renders perfectly for the medical-desert demos.

props.when_to_use: Comparison across categories where precise comparison or ranking matters.

nodes.name: area
nodes.content:
Area chart. Use to show magnitude over an ordered axis, often with cumulative emphasis, when the change over that axis matters and filled volume helps the story. Choose Area when a line would fit but showing filled magnitude or stacked contribution over an ordered axis improves the story. Avoid when precise comparison between overlapping series matters most.

Area chart — one of the seven supported chart types. Use for trend/composition over an ordered x-axis. Anti-pattern: do not use area when the task needs precise multi-series comparison (bar is better for precise comparison). Never render area on long-format data without color_col (validator rejects).

Area chart. When to use: show change over an ordered axis with emphasis on magnitude; show cumulative contribution; use stacked area when composition over an ordered axis matters more than exact series comparison. When NOT to use: users need precise comparison between overlapping series; the filled area obscures the main insight; the sequence is unordered. Required data shape: an ordered x-axis; one quantitative series or a small number of stacked series; stable intervals if applicable.

props.when_to_use: Trend or composition over an ordered x-axis; NOT when precise multi-series comparison is required.

nodes.name: pie
nodes.content:
Pie chart. When to use: show simple part-to-whole composition at one point; show a small number of slices with clearly different proportions. When NOT to use: more than a few slices; values are close and need precise comparison; the story is a trend over an ordered axis; data includes negatives or does not form one meaningful whole. Required data shape: one total divided into categories; one aggregate state; positive values only.

Pie chart. Use to show part-to-whole at one moment, when there are only a few slices and the message is composition. Choose Pie only for simple composition at a single point. Avoid when there are many slices, close values, or any trend over an ordered axis.

Pie chart — one of the seven supported chart types. Use for composition (parts of a whole) with few slices. Anti-pattern: do not use pie for too many slices.

Pie chart for composition / share of total — ONLY if <=5 segments; otherwise use a stacked column. Show percentages. Native editable PowerPoint chart.

props.when_to_use: Composition / parts-of-a-whole with few slices. Not for many slices.

nodes.name: donut
nodes.content:
Donut chart. Use to show part-to-whole with a meaningful total/label in the center, when pie logic applies and the center adds useful context. Choose Donut only for simple composition at a single point. Avoid when the center is decorative only.

Donut chart — one of the seven supported chart types. Use for composition (parts of a whole) with few slices, especially when a center total is needed. Anti-pattern: do not use donut for too many slices.

Donut chart. When to use: same part-to-whole cases as pie; prefer when the center can hold a useful total, label, or summary. When NOT to use: the center has no real informational value; slice count too high; precise slice comparison is important. Required data shape: one total divided into categories; positive values only; one aggregate state.

Donut/pie chart — use for part-of-whole composition, e.g. facility-type composition. Renders perfectly for the medical-desert demos.

props.when_to_use: Composition with few slices, especially when a center total is needed. Not for many slices.

nodes.name: bubble
nodes.content:
Bubble chart. When to use: show three quantitative dimensions in one view; compare relative position on x and y while showing scale via bubble area; highlight outliers or clusters when point count stays manageable. When NOT to use: one or more dimensions are categorical instead of numeric; so many bubbles that overlap destroys readability; users need exact reading of the size dimension. Required data shape: numeric x values; numeric y values; numeric size values encoded by bubble AREA (not diameter).

Bubble chart. Use to show three quantitative variables, when X, Y, and bubble size are all numeric and comparative. Choose Bubble only when three quantitative dimensions must be visible in one view. Avoid when one dimension is categorical or size must be read precisely.

Bubble chart — one of the seven supported chart types. Use for correlation among three quantitative dimensions (x, y, and size). Anti-pattern: do not use bubble when x, y, and size are not all quantitative.

props.when_to_use: Correlation where x, y, and size are ALL quantitative.

nodes.name: radar
nodes.content:
Radar chart. Use to compare profiles against shared bounded criteria, when all axes share the same scale and the message is profile shape. Choose Radar only when the audience needs a profile comparison across repeated criteria. Avoid when exact value reading is the priority.

Radar chart — one of the seven supported chart types. Use for profile comparison / pattern recognition across multiple dimensions. Anti-pattern: do not use radar for exact reading tasks (it is for pattern recognition, not precise value reads).

Radar chart. When to use: compare one or a few profiles across the same bounded criteria; show strengths/weaknesses across repeated dimensions; emphasize overall shape rather than exact numeric precision. When NOT to use: users need exact value comparison; too many axes or too many series; criteria do not share a consistent scale or meaning. Required data shape: a shared set of criteria; one common scale across every axis; bounded values that make the radial frame meaningful.
