props.when_to_use: Profile / multi-dimension pattern recognition. Not for exact value reading.

nodes.name: describe_dataframe
nodes.content: describe_dataframe — inspect a DataFrame's shape (schema, stats, sample rows) to determine whether data is long-format or wide-format before calling render_chart.

nodes.name: chart-selection-matrix.md
nodes.content: Bundled reference holding the chart-selection matrix used to pick among the seven supported chart types by data relationship, x-axis order, category/series count, and precision need.

props.purpose: Chart-selection matrix mapping data relationship to chart type.

nodes.name: chart-guidelines.md
nodes.content: Bundled reference holding the detailed per-chart rules: When to use, When not to use, Required data shape, Visual structure rules, Label and legend rules, Colour rules, and Anti-patterns. Always pull from this for the selected chart instead of giving generic advice.

props.purpose: Detailed per-chart-type design rules and anti-patterns.

nodes.name: data-vis-colour-rules.md
nodes.content: Bundled reference holding the data-visualisation colour and token rules: approved colour tokens, hex values, colour order, and when to use semantic colours (status encoding only).

props.purpose: Approved data-visualisation colour tokens, hex values, colour order, semantic-colour rules.

nodes.name: Reject unsupported data relationships
nodes.content: Reject all seven supported chart types (Bar, Line, Area, Pie, Donut, Bubble, Radar) if the request fundamentally needs: distribution analysis; dense correlation analysis across many points or categories; exact table-like readout; complex hierarchy; flow or process relationships; or geospatial data. In those cases, explain that the supported set does not cover the needed relationship.

props.statement: Reject all seven chart types when the request needs distribution analysis, dense correlation analysis, exact table-like readout, complex hierarchy, flow/process relationships, or geospatial data; explain that the supported set does not cover the needed relationship.

nodes.name: chart-guidelines-usage
nodes.content: Use the chart-guidelines reference document AFTER the chart-selection matrix has identified the closest chart type. It provides per-chart-type rules for when-to-use, data shape, visual structure, labels/legends, colour, and anti-patterns.

props.statement: Apply chart guidelines only after the selection matrix picks the closest chart type.

nodes.name: bar-zero-baseline
nodes.content: Bar charts MUST start the quantitative axis at zero and use a zero baseline for bar-length comparison. Anti-pattern: do not truncate the value axis away from zero. Rationale: bar length encodes the value, so a non-zero baseline distorts the comparison.

props.statement: Start the bar quantitative axis at zero; never truncate the value axis away from zero.

nodes.name: bar-orientation
nodes.content: Use vertical bars for shorter labels and common ranking views; use horizontal bars for long category names.

props.statement: Vertical bars for short labels/ranking; horizontal bars for long category names.

nodes.name: bar-grouped-vs-stacked
nodes.content: Use grouped bars only when side-by-side comparison is the main task. Use stacked bars only when composition within each category matters and exact segment comparison is secondary. Anti-pattern: do not use stacked bars when users need exact comparison across segments.

props.statement: Grouped bars for side-by-side comparison; stacked bars only when composition matters and exact segment comparison is secondary.

nodes.name: bar-category-count
nodes.content: Do not use too many categories in one bar view; bars must stay thick enough to compare confidently, and labels must remain readable. Sort intentionally, usually by value or natural category order.

props.statement: Limit bar categories so bars stay comparable and labels readable; sort by value or natural order.

nodes.name: bar-no-implied-continuity
nodes.content: Do not use bar charts to imply continuity that is better shown by a line chart.

props.statement: Do not use bars to imply continuity that a line chart shows better.

nodes.name: direct-label-vs-axis
nodes.content: Show direct labels when there are few bars/slices/points; use an axis when there are many. Use a legend only when multiple series cannot be directly labeled.

props.statement: Direct-label when few items; use an axis when many; legend only when direct labeling is impractical.

nodes.name: single-series-signal-cobalt
nodes.content: Single-series default colour is `--signal` = `#254BB2` (cobalt). Applies to bar, line, area (with a lighter fill treatment of the same colour), bubble, and radar single-series charts.

props.statement: Single-series default colour = --signal #254BB2 (cobalt).

nodes.name: categorical-sequence-strict-order
nodes.content: For multi-category / multi-series charts, use the categorical colour sequence in STRICT order: `--signal` -> `--amber` -> `--cyan` -> `--magenta` -> `--slate` -> `--mute`, then `Others` = `--grey`. Never mix arbitrary colour order; keep the sequence stable. Applies to bar, line, area (stacked), pie, donut, bubble, and radar multi-series charts.

props.statement: Multi-series colour follows the strict categorical sequence --signal -> --amber -> --cyan -> --magenta -> --slate -> --mute, then Others = --grey.

nodes.name: highlight-by-grey-track
nodes.content: For highlighted comparison in bar charts, pair `--signal #254BB2` with the `--grey #C7C2B6` neutral track/background (see data-vis-colour-rules -> highlight-by-colour).

props.statement: Highlight-by-colour: pair --signal #254BB2 with --grey #C7C2B6 neutral track.

nodes.name: others-aggregate-grey
nodes.content: Aggregate the remainder of many categories as `Others` = `--grey` `#C7C2B6`. For pie, aggregate when 6+ categories; for donut, only when an aggregated remainder is necessary.

props.statement: Aggregate excess categories as Others = --grey #C7C2B6 (pie: 6+ categories; donut: only when needed).

nodes.name: line-ordered-x-axis
nodes.content: Keep the line chart x-axis ordered and evenly interpreted; keep axis labels sparse but sufficient to preserve sequence clarity. Anti-patterns: do not use line for unordered categories; do not connect values across inconsistent or misleading intervals.

props.statement: Line x-axis must be ordered with consistent intervals; never use line for unordered categories.

nodes.name: line-markers
nodes.content: Use markers when the data is sparse or point-by-point reading matters; omit or downplay markers when the series is dense and the line is the message.

props.statement: Markers on sparse/point-readable data; downplay markers on dense lines.

nodes.name: line-direct-label-endings
nodes.content: Direct-label line endings when possible; use a legend only when direct labels would clutter the plot.

props.statement: Direct-label line endings; legend only when direct labels clutter.

nodes.name: line-limit-series
nodes.content: Limit the number of series so users can still follow each line. Anti-pattern: do not overload the chart with many series.

props.statement: Limit line series count so each line stays followable.

nodes.name: line-no-unintended-area
nodes.content: Line colour should carry the primary distinction; avoid heavy fills. Anti-pattern: do not use thick fills or styling that turns the chart into an area chart unintentionally.

props.statement: Avoid heavy fills on line charts; don't accidentally make it an area chart.

nodes.name: area-legible-top-edge
nodes.content: Keep the top edge / line boundary of an area chart legible and clear. Use subtle fill so the chart reads as data, not decoration. Anti-pattern: do not make the fill so opaque that gridlines, labels, or other series disappear.

props.statement: Area chart: keep the top edge legible, use subtle fill, never let fill hide gridlines/labels/series.

nodes.name: area-prefer-single-or-stacked
nodes.content: Prefer single-series area or stacked area over multiple overlapping filled series; keep stacking order intentional and stable across the sequence. Anti-patterns: do not use overlapping filled multi-series area for exact comparison; do not use area if a line chart communicates the trend more cleanly.

props.statement: Prefer single or stacked area over overlapping fills; keep stacking order stable.

nodes.name: area-fill-treatment
nodes.content: Single-series area default: `--signal` / `#254BB2` (cobalt) with a lighter fill treatment of the same series colour. For stacked series use the categorical sequence in strict order. Use ordered/level palettes only if stacked areas represent ordered meaning rather than arbitrary categories.

props.statement: Area fill = lighter treatment of the series colour; level palettes only for ordered stacked meaning.

nodes.name: pie-slice-count
nodes.content: Prefer 2-5 slices; keep slice count low enough that each segment stays readable; keep the circle clean and not overloaded with labels. Anti-patterns: do not use pie for many categories; do not use pie for close-value comparison needing precision; do not use pie to show change over time.

props.statement: Pie: prefer 2-5 readable slices; not for many categories, close values, or trends.

nodes.name: pie-positive-whole-only
nodes.content: Pie/donut require one total divided into categories forming one meaningful whole, with positive values only, at one moment / one aggregate state. Do not use when data includes negatives or does not form a meaningful whole.

props.statement: Pie/donut: positive values only, must form one meaningful whole at a single moment.

nodes.name: slice-order-largest-to-smallest
nodes.content: Order slices consistently, ideally largest to smallest, for pie and donut.

props.statement: Order pie/donut slices consistently, ideally largest to smallest.

nodes.name: pie-light-surface-v1
nodes.content: Keep pie slices on a light surface for v1.

props.statement: Keep pie slices on a light surface (v1).

nodes.name: donut-center-usage
nodes.content: Use the donut center only for a meaningful total, label, or title; keep center text hierarchy clear (summary first, secondary detail second); keep the center area neutral so the data ring stays dominant. Anti-pattern: do not place decorative or redundant copy in the center; do not choose donut only for style if pie communicates better.

props.statement: Donut center = meaningful total/label only, neutral, summary-first; no decorative copy.

nodes.name: donut-ring-thickness
nodes.content: Keep donut ring thickness consistent; preserve enough slice width for small categories to remain visible; keep the composition simple enough to read around the ring. Anti-pattern: do not overcrowd the ring with too many tiny slices.

props.statement: Donut: consistent ring thickness, preserve small-slice width, don't overcrowd the ring.

nodes.name: bubble-size-by-area
nodes.content: Encode the bubble size dimension by AREA, not diameter; use numeric size values. Anti-patterns: do not encode size by diameter; do not use bubble charts for exact size-comparison claims.

props.statement: Bubble size must encode by area, not diameter; never make exact size-comparison claims.

nodes.name: bubble-overlap-readability
nodes.content: Keep overlap manageable; use a size range wide enough to distinguish bubbles but not so wide that one dominates the plot; preserve visible axes and grid context for position reading. Anti-pattern: do not create dense point clouds users cannot parse.

props.statement: Bubble: keep overlap manageable, balanced size range, visible axes/grid; avoid dense point clouds.

nodes.name: bubble-label-key-points
nodes.content: Label only key bubbles or outliers; direct-label only the most important points; keep axis labels explicit because the plot carries multiple dimensions. If size needs explanation, provide a bubble-size legend or a concise note.

props.statement: Bubble: label only key/outlier points, keep axes explicit, add a size legend/note if size needs explaining.

nodes.name: bubble-colour-fourth-variable
nodes.content: Use multiple colours only when colour encodes a separate categorical grouping and the point count stays readable; follow the categorical sequence in strict order if used. Anti-pattern: do not introduce colour as a fourth variable unless it genuinely helps.

props.statement: Bubble: add colour only as a genuine categorical 4th variable (strict sequence), not gratuitously.

nodes.name: radar-axis-and-series-limit
nodes.content: Keep the number of radar axes small enough to read comfortably and keep series count low; preserve the radial grid so users can estimate the profile shape. Anti-pattern: do not overload with many axes or series.

props.statement: Radar: few axes, low series count, preserve the radial grid for shape estimation.

nodes.name: radar-shared-scale
nodes.content: Radar requires a shared set of criteria on one common scale across every axis; state the shared scale if not obvious from context. Anti-pattern: do not compare criteria that are not truly parallel or do not share a scale.

props.statement: Radar criteria must share one common scale across all axes; state it if not obvious; never compare non-parallel criteria.

nodes.name: radar-subtle-fill-clear-outline
nodes.content: Use subtle fill and a clear/visible outline on radar charts; keep axis labels short. Anti-pattern: do not use radar for precise value comparisons (emphasize shape, not exact numbers).

props.statement: Radar: subtle fill, clear outline, short axis labels; emphasize shape over exact values.

nodes.name: datavis_tokens_single_source
nodes.content: All data-vis colours come from `tokens.css` (the single source of truth, reconciled RA-editorial). The engine `compose_infographic` applies them automatically — you do NOT pass colours. Use only the `tokens.css` data-vis tokens and their exact hex values; no colour tokens or hexes outside `tokens.css`.

props.statement: Data-vis colours come from tokens.css only; the compose_infographic engine applies them automatically — never pass colours, never use hexes outside tokens.css.

nodes.name: no_bright_product_palette
nodes.content: Bright product/brand palettes are NOT used in the data-vis system — saturated reds (e.g. #E00842), lavenders, and consumer health-app accent colours MUST NOT be used in the hackathon's RA-branded stories. Stick to the cobalt + warm-neutral editorial palette.

props.statement: Do not use bright product/brand palettes (saturated reds like #E00842, lavenders, consumer-app accents) in RA-branded stories; stick to the cobalt + warm-neutral editorial palette.

nodes.name: light_editorial_canvas
nodes.content: Light theme only — warm-neutral editorial canvas: `--paper #FAF6EE` / `--oat #F5EFE3`. The subject (people unable to reach healthcare) is serious: muted palette, no bright reds, no rainbow scales.

props.statement: Light theme only on a warm-neutral canvas (--paper #FAF6EE / --oat #F5EFE3); the serious subject demands a muted palette with no bright reds or rainbow scales.

nodes.name: default_single_series_signal
nodes.content: General single-series charts use the brand primary `--signal` = `#254BB2` (cobalt) — the lead line, single-category bars, and the series that carries the message.

props.statement: Default single-series colour is --signal #254BB2 (cobalt): lead line, single-category bars, the message-carrying series.

nodes.name: neutral_supporting_grey
nodes.content: `--grey` = `#C7C2B6` is the neutral supporting colour for tracks, backgrounds, and de-emphasized comparison items. It is also the default for EVERY series that is NOT the highlighted one (see Highlight-by-colour).

props.statement: --grey #C7C2B6 is the neutral supporting colour for tracks/backgrounds/de-emphasized items and the default for every non-highlighted series.

nodes.name: categorical_sequence_order
nodes.content: For multi-category charts, apply colours strictly left-to-right in this exact order (mirrors `_SERIES` in the engine): 1. `--signal` #254BB2 (cobalt), 2. `--amber` #DF9B44, 3. `--cyan` #2695AC, 4. `--magenta` #913F82, 5. `--slate` #3A3A40, 6. `--mute` #9CA0A3, 7. Others = `--grey` #C7C2B6 (aggregate the remainder). For more than 6 distinct categories, aggregate the remainder as 'Others' (--grey). Keep the sequence stable across related charts on the same page when categories match.

props.statement: Categorical sequence in exact left-to-right order: signal #254BB2, amber #DF9B44, cyan #2695AC, magenta #913F82, slate #3A3A40, mute #9CA0A3, then Others=grey #C7C2B6 (aggregate beyond 6); keep stable across related charts.

nodes.name: highlight_by_colour
nodes.content:
Most series render neutral grey; only the entity the sentence names gets an accent -- set scene.highlight to that entity's label (string) or labels (array). The accent is cobalt #254BB2 (amber on dark backgrounds). This is why the eye lands where the prose points -- don't rainbow-colour everything. You direct it (set highlight); the engine enforces it (you don't pass colours). Palette + type come from tokens.css: oat/ivory canvas, cobalt primary, amber/cyan/magenta accents, Source Serif 4 display / Manrope / JetBrains Mono.

Highlight-by-colour is the default storytelling mode: most series render neutral `--grey`; only the entity the prose names gets an accent (`--signal`, or `--amber` on dark backgrounds). Set `scene.highlight` to that entity. This makes the eye land where the sentence points — prefer it over giving every series its own colour.

Highlight-by-colour rule: render neutral P.grey by default; only names in scene.highlight (string or array) get P.signal via H.hue, thicker stroke, larger dots, plus value labels + name label + the rank annotation. Neutrals stay quiet (no labels) to keep the crossing legible — matches the reference, which labelled only Italy.

props.statement: Default storytelling mode: render most series neutral --grey and accent only the prose-named entity (--signal, or --amber on dark bg) via scene.highlight; prefer over per-series colour.

nodes.name: sequential_cobalt_ramp
nodes.content: Sequential palette: a single-hue light→dark COBALT ramp (`#EAF0FB` → `#254BB2`), used by the choropleth and heatmap. Use a colour ramp ONLY when the meaning is genuinely ordered (rate, magnitude band, risk, progress) — never for arbitrary categories. Lightness/saturation are perceptually ordered; hue is not.

props.statement: Sequential = single-hue cobalt ramp #EAF0FB→#254BB2 (choropleth, heatmap); use ramps only for genuinely ordered measures (rate/magnitude/risk/progress), never arbitrary categories.

nodes.name: diverging_amber_cobalt
nodes.content: Diverging palette: amber ↔ cobalt with a pale middle — use ONLY when the measure crosses a meaningful midpoint (e.g. a deviation around 0). Never red↔green (colour-blind unsafe).

props.statement: Diverging = amber↔cobalt with a pale middle, only for measures crossing a meaningful midpoint (deviation around 0); never red↔green (colour-blind unsafe).

nodes.name: print_number_when_colour_encodes_value
nodes.content: Where colour encodes a number AND precision matters (the matrix/heatmap), print the number in the cell — colour is a low-rank perceptual channel (Cleveland–McGill), so it is secondary to the printed value.

props.statement: When colour encodes a number and precision matters (heatmap matrix), print the number in the cell — colour is a low-rank Cleveland–McGill channel, secondary to the value.

nodes.name: no_rainbow_no_bright_red
nodes.content: Avoid rainbow / saturated palettes and bright reds (the subject is grave). Do not use semantic UI colours as a default chart palette.

props.statement: No rainbow/saturated palettes, no bright reds (grave subject), and no semantic UI colours as a default chart palette.

nodes.name: no_pie_no_3d_use_ranked_bar
nodes.content: No 3-D and no pie/donut (low-rank angle/area encodings) — use `ranked_bar` or `kpi_grid` instead.

props.statement: Avoid 3-D and pie/donut (low-rank angle/area encodings); use ranked_bar or kpi_grid instead.

nodes.name: stable_category_colour_order
nodes.content: No randomised category-colour order — keep the categorical sequence stable across related charts.

props.statement: Never randomise category-colour order; keep the categorical sequence stable across related charts.

nodes.name: dont_rely_on_colour_alone
nodes.content: Don't rely on colour alone to distinguish categories — pair colour with label and position (~8% of men have a colour-vision deficiency). Accessibility requirement.

props.statement: Don't distinguish categories by colour alone — pair with label/position (~8% of men have a colour-vision deficiency).

nodes.name: count_up
nodes.content: Variant of stat: one headline number that animates a count-up. Encoding: text. Use for a single weighty number. See stat recipe.

props.when_to_use: One headline number with weight (animated count-up)

nodes.name: cleveland-mcgill-encoding-hierarchy
nodes.content: Pick the archetype by the QUESTION using the Cleveland-McGill (1984) perceptual hierarchy: decoding accuracy falls position on a common scale > length > angle/area > colour/shading. Prefer position/length encodings; reserve colour for categories or as a secondary channel where the number is also printed (heatmap). When two archetypes fit, prefer the higher-rank encoding (ranked_bar over a donut for composition; dumbbell over two separate bars for a 2-state gap). Never use 3-D, never a pie/donut (angle+area are low-rank -- use ranked_bar or kpi_grid).

props.statement: Pick by the question; prefer position/length over angle/area/colour (Cleveland-McGill 1984); never 3-D, pie, or donut.

nodes.name: number-formatting
nodes.content: Numbers in prose + stat-card values: 14.34 million for >=1M; 1,109,357 (commas) for 100K-1M; bare for <100K; 94.06% not 0.9406; signed deltas +12.4% / -3.1%. The charts' axis/value labels auto-format K/M/B -- you only format prose + stat-card values.

props.statement: Prose/stat-card number formatting: '14.34 million' >=1M; commas 100K-1M; bare <100K; 94.06% not 0.9406; signed deltas. Chart axes auto-format K/M/B.

nodes.name: reframe-editorial-spine
nodes.content: Every strong story shows the second number behind the first. Lead with the reframe in the lede and the hero stat; state it plainly. Canonical India medical-desert reframes: a headline 'X facilities' count -> iceberg (most districts have NO recorded facility in this ~10k sample); 'high-burden districts are under-served' -> slope/dumbbell + bubble_scatter (the raw supply-vs-need correlation is CONFOUNDED by urbanisation — once an urban proxy is controlled the effect vanishes, R-confounder-urbanisation); a state HBI leaderboard -> the district access gap (Bihar's Araria belt vs Kerala); 'every district is roughly comparable' -> heatmap_matrix (district x indicator, access varies up to ~80x).

props.statement: The reframe spine: show the second number behind the first; lead with it in lede + hero stat. iceberg/slope/dumbbell/bubble_scatter/heatmap_matrix carry the India medical-desert reframes (zero-facility districts, urbanisation-confound, district access gap).

nodes.name: report-story-structure
nodes.content: A story (>=2 scenes) is more than a chart dump -- sequence it: (1) Kicker + serif title + lede (the headline finding, human framing first); (2) Hero stat cards via stats= (2-3 numbers, leading with the reframe -- the second number standing behind the first); (3) Scene panels in narrative order, each a titled chart that advances the argument with an eyebrow ('SECTION · 0N'), a one-line lede, and a caption that voices the finding; (4) Methodology -- period, definitions, controls, limitations, source.

props.statement: Report story structure: kicker+title+lede -> hero stat cards (lead with the reframe) -> ordered scene panels (eyebrow/lede/caption) -> methodology.

nodes.name: motion-final-geometry-first
nodes.content: The compose_infographic scene engine draws FINAL GEOMETRY FIRST and fades in with a CSS animation (animation-fill-mode: both) -- reliable under screenshots and backgrounded tabs, and it honours prefers-reduced-motion. You don't manage animation for infographics; opacity-only fade is the whole story there. (Contrast: compose_story IS scroll-driven -- a static sticky chart that only fades in on load is a bug, not a safe default.)

props.statement: compose_infographic: final geometry first + opacity-only CSS fade (fill-mode both), reduced-motion honoured, no animation management. compose_story is the opposite (scroll-driven).

nodes.name: motion-is-the-scroll
nodes.content: For compose_story the sticky chart is a state machine, not a one-shot drawing -- THE MOTION IS THE SCROLL (non-negotiable). Each chapter's renderer render(svg, ch, ci, si) draws the chart for step si, called on load (si=0) and again on every step change, interpolating with a d3 transition; that swap is the animation. Scaffold wiring to keep: renderBars/renderLine build geometry once (keyed .data() joins) then T(sel)-transition on later calls (T() is a real transition live, no-op under prefers-reduced-motion); an IntersectionObserver marks the active .step and re-invokes the chapter's renderer with the new si (that line is the swap -- never delete it); per-step choreography lives in data (each step may carry view:{highlight, reveal, annotate:{at,text}}; omit view and the highlight still walks). New chart types add a renderer the same way ((svg,ch,ci,si), keyed joins, T()) and register in RENDER -- do NOT fall back to a draw-once static chart. Reliability: step 0 must render the complete chart; every per-step change goes through T() so reduced-motion/export paths stay static. Verify: scrolling a chapter must visibly change its chart, not just dim the prose.

props.statement: compose_story: sticky chart is a state machine; renderer render(svg,ch,ci,si) re-invoked per step via IntersectionObserver, swaps geometry through T() (no-op under reduced motion). Step 0 = complete chart. Verify scroll visibly changes the chart.

nodes.name: data-injection-no-hand-typed-figures
nodes.content: No figure is hand-typed; everything is computed -- mirrors the validated build pipeline (notebooks -> findings.json -> inject at '__DATA__'). Scenes get data two ways: variable_name + mapping (tool shapes the slice from a stored DataFrame, for SQL-friendly archetypes) OR inline data (precomputed in run_python_code, required for statistical archetypes). compose_story injects the data dict at the '__DATA__' token and the palette at '__PALETTE__'; no number may be hand-typed in the template. Read recipes/<type>.md before composing a scene that needs inline data.

props.statement: Never hand-type figures; compute via mapping (SQL-friendly) or inline data in run_python_code. compose_story injects data at __DATA__ / palette at __PALETTE__. Read recipes/<type>.md first.

nodes.name: infographics-anti-patterns
nodes.content: Anti-patterns: (1) Pie/donut/3-D anything (low-rank -- use ranked_bar/kpi_grid). (2) A chart for a single number (use stat, or just say it). (3) Rainbow/saturated palettes (subject is serious; cobalt+neutral, accent sparingly). (4) Hand-typed figures in a story (compute everything; pass via data/mapping). (5) Top-N leaderboard with sentinel rows (filter out unknown/placeholder district codes). (6) Pasting the infographic URL or a fabricated Volumes link in chat (reference by title). (7) Reporting the raw supply-vs-need correlation as causal — it is CONFOUNDED by urbanisation (R-confounder-urbanisation); report the controlled coefficient. (8) Treating facility counts as a census — it is a ~10k SAMPLE (coverage, not supply); no per-capita denominator exists in these tables. (9) compose_story for a standard chart (that's compose_infographic's job).

props.statement: Anti-patterns: no pie/donut/3-D; no chart for one number; no rainbow; no hand-typed figures; filter sentinel district codes; never paste url/Volumes link; don't report the urbanisation-confounded supply-vs-need correlation as causal; facilities is a sample not a census, no per-capita; don't use compose_story for a standard chart.

nodes.name: source-footer
nodes.content: Source footer always: 'Virtue Foundation dataset — facilities, india_post_pincode_directory, nfhs_5_district_health_indicators (NFHS-5 district health indicators)'. Note in the footer that facilities is a ~10k SAMPLE (coverage, not a census) and facility claims are self-reported. Surface only the title in prose; reference the infographic by title, never paste the url or a databricksapps.com/Volumes link.

props.statement: Always footer the Virtue Foundation dataset (facilities / india_post_pincode_directory / nfhs_5_district_health_indicators), noting the ~10k sample + self-reported caveat; reference by title, never paste url/Volumes link.

nodes.name: recipes/_flagship_scaffold.html
nodes.content: RA-branded scrollytelling skeleton HTML scaffold. Starting point for compose_story (the freehand flagship escape hatch): copy it as template_html, compute the data dict in run_python_code, pass template_html + data. Already wires the state-machine end-to-end (renderBars/renderLine keyed joins + T() transitions, IntersectionObserver step swap, per-step view choreography). Injection tokens: '__DATA__' (data dict) and optional '__PALETTE__' (brand palette).

props.purpose: RA-branded scrollytelling scaffold for compose_story; injection tokens __DATA__ / __PALETTE__

nodes.name: recipes/
nodes.content: Per-archetype recipe directory: recipes/<type>.md holds the exact data shape, a sample, and a copy-paste compute snippet for each statistical archetype (forest_ci, bubble_scatter, heatmap_matrix, choropleth, dumbbell, slope, pyramid, bar_race, iceberg, projection, sankey_corridors, kpi_grid) and the SQL-friendly ones. Read the recipe before composing that scene.

props.purpose: Per-archetype data shape + sample + copy-paste compute snippet; read before composing the scene

nodes.name: data-visualisation-chart-guidance
nodes.content: Chart-choice theory reference directory (../data-visualisation-chart-guidance/): Cleveland-McGill hierarchy, proportional ink, colour encoding, no-3D. Grounds the archetype-by-question selection.

props.purpose: Chart-choice theory: Cleveland-McGill hierarchy, proportional ink, colour encoding, no-3D

nodes.name: stacked_bar_100pct
nodes.content: Single 100%-width horizontal stacked bar split into two segments by share. Used by the iceberg recipe: 'above' segment starts at x=0, 'below' stacks immediately after, widths set from share = value/(above+below). White % labels rendered inside each segment.

props.when_to_use: When two parts of a whole must be shown as proportions of a fixed-width bar (e.g. visible tip vs hidden mass in the iceberg recipe)

nodes.name: iceberg-reduced-motion-safe
nodes.content: Iceberg MOTION rule: the recipe is fully reduced-motion / screenshot safe. Every rect has its FINAL width set at t=0; the dashed waterline has its dash array set immediately (NO stroke-dashoffset reveal, unlike the reference which animated the line drawing on); all motion is opacity-only via H.in. Rects use solid fill (no fill-opacity attr) so H.in's style('opacity') animation is the only opacity channel and nothing is clobbered.

props.statement: Iceberg renders screenshot-safe: final rect widths at t=0, dash array set immediately (no dashoffset reveal), opacity-only motion via H.in over solid-fill rects.

nodes.name: iceberg-highlight-signal-cobalt
nodes.content: Iceberg HIGHLIGHT rule: default (no scene.highlight) renders the above-waterline tip in signal cobalt and the below mass in neutral grey, with the below annotation also in signal to point at the hidden bulk (mirrors the reference's clay-tip/blue-mass contrast within the RA palette). If scene.highlight is set, it matches against above.label / below.label and only the named segment gets the signal accent (everything else grey); the below annotation then drops to slate.

props.statement: Default highlight: above tip + below annotation in signal cobalt, below mass grey. With scene.highlight set, only the named segment is signal (rest grey) and the below annotation drops to slate.

nodes.name: iceberg-palette-P-plus-white-labels
nodes.content: Iceberg PALETTE rule: uses only existing CSS classes (vlabel, annot, axis-title) and only P palette colours + '#fff' for in-bar labels (matches the reference's white-on-bar value labels and the other renderers' .attr('fill','#fff') pattern). No <style> injected, no extra CDN. viewBox 720x420 (wide chart) consistent with line_multi/stacked_area.

props.statement: Iceberg uses only P-palette colours + '#fff' for in-bar labels, existing classes (vlabel/annot/axis-title), no injected <style>, no extra CDN, viewBox 720x420.

nodes.name: build_flagship.py
nodes.content:
Reference Python build script containing the original renderIceberg renderer that the iceberg recipe was ported from. The reference used a multi-segment bar with the waterline at a category boundary and animated the waterline line-drawing; the recipe collapses that to the 2-segment {above,below} visible-tip vs hidden-mass contract (same reframe, same waterline position) and makes it screenshot-safe.

Reference Python build script. Contains the original Simpson's-paradox slopegraph renderer that the 'slope' recipe is a faithful port of into the scene-engine contract.

props.purpose: Reference renderIceberg implementation the iceberg recipe was ported and simplified from

nodes.name: stat_card
nodes.content: Big-number stat-card primitive: a large value with a descriptive label, optional signed delta with directional arrow (▲ up / ▼ down), and an optional dim sub-context line. Rendered as flat HTML (not SVG) so text stays crisp; the kpi_grid recipe tiles many of these into an auto-fit grid. Default value colour cobalt (P.signal); highlighted card uses its accent; delta up=amber, down=alarm.

props.when_to_use: Headline a single key metric as a large number with optional delta and context; tile multiple via kpi_grid for a KPI dashboard.

nodes.name: kpi_grid_palette_zero_new_colours
nodes.content: kpi_grid draws ALL colours and fonts from the existing CSS custom properties (var(--serif/--sans/--mono)) and the P palette — zero new colours, no injected <style>. Values are cobalt (P.signal) by default; a card whose label is in scene.highlight gets its accent. Delta colours: up = P.amber, down = P.alarm (sober editorial palette). Delta arrows use Unicode geometric triangles (▲/▼), never emoji.

props.statement: Use only P palette + CSS custom properties (var(--serif/sans/mono)); cobalt P.signal default value, accent on highlighted card, delta up=amber/down=alarm; ▲/▼ triangles not emoji; no new colours, no injected <style>.

nodes.name: kpi_grid_screenshot_safe_motion
nodes.content: kpi_grid motion is screenshot-safe: each card's full text is painted immediately, then H.in fades opacity only (staggered). Under reduced-motion or a backgrounded/rasterized tab the final values are already present at t=0. No width/transform reveals. HTML-not-SVG by design — appends HTML divs for crisp text (does NOT call H.svg); .figure.bare CSS strips the card chrome so the grid sits flat on the page, matching the page-level stat / .stat-card renderer.

props.statement: Paint full card text at t=0 then fade opacity only (staggered, reduced-motion/rasterize-safe); no width/transform reveals; render flat HTML with .figure.bare (not SVG/H.svg).

nodes.name: pyramid two-tone female=signal/male=slate is intrinsic
nodes.content: On the pyramid recipe the two-toned encoding female=P.signal (cobalt primary) / male=P.slate is intrinsic — it IS the chart's meaning — and is NOT overridden by scene.highlight to a single colour. scene.highlight only DIMS one side: 'female' dims the male side (fill-opacity 0.30), 'male' dims the female side; with no highlight both sides render at fill-opacity 0.92 (reference resting state). Mapped from the reference's clay(female)/blue(male) palette into the engine palette.

props.statement: Two-tone female=signal/male=slate is the encoding; highlight only dims one side, never collapses to a single colour.

nodes.name: final-geometry-first motion survives headless screenshot
nodes.content: Renderer reliability rule applied in the pyramid recipe: final geometry is set immediately on every rect; H.in animates ONLY element opacity (style). The dim effect uses the fill-opacity ATTR so it survives — H.in owns element 'opacity', so the two compose (element-opacity fade x fill-opacity dim) without clobbering. A backgrounded tab / headless-Chrome screenshot at t=0 shows full correct geometry.

props.statement: Set final geometry immediately; animate only element opacity; use fill-opacity attr for dim so t=0 screenshots render correctly.

nodes.name: slope
nodes.content: Slopegraph primitive: two parallel x-positions (d3.scalePoint), one line per entity connecting a left value to a right value, with endpoint circles at both ends and a value y-axis. Picks when comparing the same set of entities across exactly two conditions/measures and the change-in-rank between them is the message (crossing lines).

props.when_to_use: compare same entities across two measures/conditions where rank change (crossing lines) is the message

nodes.name: compose_infographic.py
nodes.content: Implementation of the compose_infographic tool. Holds _shape_scene_data() which dispatches per-scene-type data shaping; the slope shaper is NOT yet wired in — to enable DataFrame-driven slope scenes add a `t == "slope"` branch calling the slope shaper body. Inline scene.data already works via the existing `scene.get('data') is not None` short-circuit.

props.purpose: compose_infographic tool implementation; _shape_scene_data() dispatch (slope branch not yet added)

nodes.name: final-geometry-first-motion
nodes.content: Motion-compliance rule: draw all final geometry (lines, circles, labels) immediately at correct coordinates; animate OPACITY only via H.in. Partial transparency on neutral lines must use the stroke-opacity ATTR (0.7), not style opacity, so H.in's element-opacity tween never clobbers it. Result is correct at t=0 under headless-Chrome rasterization and reduced-motion.

props.statement: All final geometry drawn at correct coords immediately; only opacity animates (H.in). Use stroke-opacity ATTR for partial transparency, never style opacity, so the opacity tween doesn't clobber it. Correct at t=0 for headless-Chrome and reduced-motion.

nodes.name: no-injected-style-existing-classes
nodes.content: CSS rule: use only existing classes (axis, vlabel, clabel, annot) and CSS vars / P hexes; do NOT inject a <style> block. For slope, the value y-axis is assumed 0..1 fractions when y_format='pct'; pass y_format:'num' plus a value_label scene field for absolute numbers.

props.statement: Use only existing classes (axis, vlabel, clabel, annot) + CSS vars/P hexes; no injected <style>. y_format='pct' => 0..1 y-axis; y_format='num' needs a value_label field.

nodes.name: forest_plot
nodes.content: Forest plot: point estimate (dot) + horizontal whisker (confidence interval) per row, typically on a log x-axis with a dashed reference line at the null value (OR=1). Pick when displaying effect-size estimates with uncertainty across several categories and a baseline.

props.when_to_use: Display effect-size estimates (odds/risk ratios) with 95% CIs across multiple categories against a reference baseline.

nodes.name: neutral by default accent only the named entity
nodes.content: Default rows render P.grey; the reference row (ref:true) renders P.ink + bold (the fixed OR=1 baseline, not a measured estimate); only scene.highlight gets the accent (P.signal). The original tri-colour EU/EFTA/reference scheme was folded into the engine's highlight-by-colour rule -- keeping the 'neutral by default, accent only the named entity' rule while preserving the visually-distinct reference baseline.

props.statement: Render rows neutral (P.grey) by default; accent only the highlighted entity (P.signal); the reference OR=1 row is ink+bold as a fixed baseline.

nodes.name: final-geometry-first opacity-only motion
nodes.content:
Renderer reliability rule: every element draws at FINAL geometry immediately (cx/cy/r and line endpoints set up front); only opacity fades in via H.in, so a screenshot at t=0 shows correct geometry. Partial bubble transparency uses the fill-opacity ATTR (not style opacity, which H.in owns). Bubble fade delay is capped Math.min(i*18,700) so many points don't stagger past ~0.7s.

All geometry (whiskers, dots, labels) is drawn at FINAL position immediately; only opacity is animated, per-row, via H.in on the row <g>. A t=0 screenshot / backgrounded tab shows the complete plot. CI whisker transparency uses the stroke-opacity ATTR (.55), not style opacity, so it never collides with H.in's element-opacity animation.

props.statement: Draw all geometry at final position immediately; animate only opacity (per-row via H.in) so a t=0 screenshot or backgrounded tab shows the complete plot; use stroke-opacity attr for whisker transparency to avoid colliding with the opacity animation.

nodes.name: build_forest.py
nodes.content: Reference Python source that the forest_ci recipe is a faithful port of -- defines the log x-axis, point dot + 95% CI whisker per row, dashed OR=1 reference line with '<ref> = 1' annotation, caseload-scaled dot radius (scaleSqrt), mono OR value label at the whisker high end, and the stacked legend + axis title layout.

props.purpose: Original forest-plot renderer that forest_ci ports to the infographic engine.

nodes.name: choropleth
nodes.content: Choropleth — a filled-region geographic map where each region is shaded by a numeric value via a sequential colour ramp. Pick it to show one metric varying across geography keyed by a numeric feature id (e.g. facility coverage or an NFHS-5 indicator by district/state). TopoJSON features are keyed by their numeric feature.id.

props.when_to_use: show a single numeric metric varying across geography keyed by a numeric feature id

nodes.name: world-atlas countries-110m.json
nodes.content: world-atlas TopoJSON atlas (default countries-110m.json) fetched at RUNTIME via d3.json from CDN — NOT a <script>, so NOT in extra_cdn. Features are keyed by feature.id = ISO-3166-1 numeric code. Override with d.atlas_url if mirrored. If the CDN fetch fails (or topojson is missing) the renderer paints a graceful red annotation message instead of throwing.

props.purpose: country boundary geometry for the choropleth map, keyed by numeric ISO

nodes.name: topojson-client
nodes.content: topojson-client — the ONLY extra_cdn library added for the choropleth recipe (d3 is already in the infographic _SCAFFOLD). Used to convert the world-atlas TopoJSON into GeoJSON features for rendering. If topojson is missing the renderer paints a graceful red annotation instead of throwing.

props.purpose: TopoJSON->GeoJSON conversion for the choropleth map

nodes.name: bar_race_motion_reliability
nodes.content: Bar-race motion/reliability contract: the LATEST frame is painted first as the resting geometry (correct at t=0 for headless-Chrome rasterization and backgrounded tabs), then opacity-faded in via H.in. Bar-race is the contract's explicit 'inherently animated' exception, so the replay loop (earliest→latest, ending on latest) recomputes bar widths per frame via a d3.timer — width/transform changes happen ONLY inside the loop, never as the reveal mechanism. RM (prefers-reduced-motion) short-circuits the loop, leaving the static final frame. The replay starts after a 900ms delay so a screenshot taken near t=0 captures the final-frame resting state, not frame 0. opacity is controlled via attr('opacity',...) on the .row groups (H.in owns element 'opacity' on the gBars/yearMark wrappers); partial transparency uses fill-opacity per the contract.

props.statement: Paint the latest frame first as resting geometry; replay loop (after a 900ms delay) recomputes bar widths per frame; width/transform changes only inside the loop, never as the reveal; prefers-reduced-motion leaves the static final frame.

nodes.name: bar_race_css_classes
nodes.content: Bar-race uses ONLY contract CSS classes (no <style> injected): 'axis' (gridline ticks via axisTop with tickSize(-ih)), 'axis-title', 'clabel' (in-bar entity label, white on wide bars / ink when label sits outside), 'vlabel' (mono value to the right of each bar), 'annot' (one optional italic-serif finding from scene.annotations[0]). The large background year numeral has NO matching class in the scaffold, so it is styled inline only (var(--serif), grey at 55% fill-opacity) — the single inline-style exception.

props.statement: Use only contract classes axis / axis-title / clabel / vlabel / annot; the background year numeral is the one inline-styled element (var(--serif), grey 55% fill-opacity), no <style> injected.

nodes.name: bar_race_highlight_by_colour
nodes.content: Bar-race highlight rule: by default every bar is neutral (H.hue → P.grey) and only the label equal to scene.highlight gets P.signal (the sober highlight-one-entity default). If the python_shaper is given mapping['color_col'] (e.g. a region-bucket hex like the reference's BUCKET_COLOR), per-row d.frames[*].rows[*].color overrides and colours bars by region — reproducing the reference's editorial region palette. Leave color_col unset for the sober default.

props.statement: Default: all bars neutral P.grey, scene.highlight gets P.signal. Set mapping['color_col'] to colour bars by region via per-row hex; leave unset for highlight-one-entity.

nodes.name: build_race.py
nodes.content: The original reference implementation that bar_race is a faithful port of (adapted to the scene-engine contract). The recipe matches build_race.py's render() — the renderer does the top_n slice + per-frame re-rank itself over the full per-year field. Simplifications vs the reference: dropped the interactive play/pause/scrub/speed UI and linear inter-year interpolation (scene engine renders static-or-autoplay, not a controllable widget); the leader caption + takeaway cards + legend live in scene narrative fields (lede/caption/annotations) rather than chart chrome.

props.purpose: original bar chart race implementation ported into the bar_race scene-engine recipe

nodes.name: sankey
nodes.content: Sankey flow diagram: nodes on two sides (sources left, destinations right) connected by bands whose width encodes a value (here a count per source->destination flow). Pick when showing directed left->right flows from sources to destinations and the long tail can be bundled. Requires the d3-sankey CDN (d3.sankey / d3.sankeyLinkHorizontal attached onto global d3 when the UMD bundle loads after d3 core).

props.when_to_use: directed source->destination flow magnitudes; stacks rows vertically so needs a taller viewBox

nodes.name: ra_cobalt_palette_highlight_by_colour
nodes.content: RA cobalt palette + highlight-by-colour: Sankey links are coloured by their SOURCE node; the single biggest source defaults to P.signal (cobalt accent), everything else P.grey. scene.highlight names which source(s) get accented. The reference's clay/region palette is deliberately replaced by this RA cobalt palette. The bundled 'all other' band is faint grey and excluded from accent colouring.

props.statement: links coloured by SOURCE node; biggest/selected source = P.signal cobalt, rest = P.grey; faint-grey 'other' band excluded from accent; RA cobalt replaces reference clay/region palette

nodes.name: allow_list_label_classes
nodes.content: Headers and labels use the engine's allow-list classes (axis-title / clabel / vlabel / annot) instead of the reference's bespoke colhead/nodelabel/nodeval classes. Origin labels sit left of the rect with value below, host labels right, plus column headers and a finding annotation for the biggest single corridor.

props.statement: use allow-list classes axis-title/clabel/vlabel/annot, not bespoke colhead/nodelabel/nodeval

nodes.name: motion_safety_final_geometry_first
nodes.content: MOTION SAFETY for sankey_corridors: final link path 'd' + stroke-width and node rect width/height are set immediately; the only animation is H.in (opacity). Verified via jsdom that every path has a valid 'd' and stroke-width>=1 and every rect has positive geometry at t=0, so headless-Chrome rasterization and backgrounded tabs show the correct Sankey. Partial transparency uses stroke-opacity / fill-opacity ATTRS (not style.opacity) so H.in's element-opacity animation does not clobber them. Under reduced-motion H.in is a no-op and geometry stays put. Reveal is a largest-first opacity-only stagger.

props.statement: set final geometry (path d, stroke-width, rect w/h) at t=0; animate only opacity via H.in; use stroke/fill-opacity attrs not style.opacity; reduced-motion = no-op

nodes.name: sober_voice_no_emoji
nodes.content: VOICE: sober, no emoji. Tooltips and annotations name the flow plainly (e.g. 'facilities'). Keep any category distinctions upstream — the shaper does no category mixing; pass it a single clean flow frame.

props.statement: sober, no emoji; tooltips/annotation name the flow plainly; single clean flow frame, no category mixing in the shaper

nodes.name: scatter
nodes.content: Scatter plot primitive: points positioned on two continuous axes. The bubble_scatter recipe is a log-log scatter variant where bubble area encodes a third quantity (e.g. sample size) and colour encodes a categorical region series.

props.when_to_use: Show the relationship between two continuous variables across many entities; add bubble-size for a 3rd quantity and a fitted trend line

nodes.name: colour-encodes-region-not-highlight
nodes.content: In bubble_scatter, COLOUR ENCODES REGION via d3.scaleOrdinal over the SERIES (the `regions` array), NOT the engine's usual highlight-by-colour. scene.highlight is repurposed: it only ADDS a label to the named point(s), on top of any point with note:true. For zero region colouring use a different archetype.

props.statement: Bubble colour is a categorical region encoding (ordinal scale over `regions`), not a highlight; scene.highlight only adds labels, never recolours

nodes.name: annotation-anchored-top-left-no-collision
nodes.content: The r/R² finding annotation is anchored top-left of the plot (x=8, y=14) so it never collides with the regression line or the right-gutter region legends.

props.statement: Anchor the r/R² annotation top-left (x=8,y=14) to avoid collision with the fit line and right-gutter legends

nodes.name: editorial-finding-must-match-data
nodes.content: bubble_scatter ships the reference's editorial finding "significant, but practically negligible" (calibrated to R²≈0.05). If the agent's data shows a strong fit this sentence is WRONG — gate it or let the agent override via scene.caption. Left as-is to match the validated story.

props.statement: The 'significant but practically negligible' finding wording is calibrated to R²≈0.05; override via scene.caption if the fit is strong

nodes.name: bubble-scatter-css-class-remap
nodes.content: CSS-class remap vs the build_bubble.py reference (classes that don't exist in _SCAFFOLD): albl→clabel (sans, ink, 600 — visually identical), legend→clabel at 11px, r/R² finding→annot (italic serif), axis titles→axis-title (engine class is mono/uppercase so titles render UPPERCASED — a minor change from the reference's sentence-case .axt; acceptable and on-brand). Bubble stroke = P.paper (canvas) for halo separation (reference used --ivory).

props.statement: Remap reference classes to engine classes (albl→clabel, legend→clabel 11px, finding→annot, axis→axis-title which uppercases); bubble stroke = P.paper for halo

nodes.name: build_bubble.py
nodes.content: Reference Python build script that the bubble_scatter recipe is a faithful port of. Holds the original region-ramp colouring, motion, annotation placement, and CSS classes (albl/legend/.axt, --ivory paper) that the recipe remaps to engine equivalents.

props.purpose: Source-of-truth reference port for the bubble_scatter D3 build (colour ramp, motion, annotation, CSS classes)

nodes.name: final-geometry-at-t0
nodes.content: Non-negotiable motion contract rule: dropped stroke-dashoffset animation — connectors have FINAL geometry at t=0 and reveal via H.in (opacity) only; rows fade via H.in; annotations fade via H.in with a later delay. A t=0 screenshot must show fully-correct geometry. Partial transparency on connectors uses the stroke-opacity ATTR (.85), not style opacity, so H.in's element-opacity doesn't clobber it.

props.statement: No layout-changing motion: render final geometry at t=0, reveal via opacity (H.in) only; connectors keep correct geometry from frame 0.

nodes.name: mover-direction-colour
nodes.content: Dumbbell colour rule: default grey (P.grey). Movers get direction colour: up -> P.amber, down -> P.cyan (the reference used clay/blue; mapped to the engine's amber/cyan accents). scene.highlight (name OR key, string or array) overrides to accent any entity with P.signal. Standouts (on only one column, e.g. a district ranked high on raw need only, or high once urbanisation-adjusted only) are data-driven via standout:true.

props.statement: Movers coloured by direction (up=amber, down=cyan); default grey; scene.highlight overrides to P.signal accent.

nodes.name: heatmap
nodes.content:
A grid/matrix of cells where a value is encoded across two categorical axes by colour intensity (and here also a printed number). Pick when comparing one metric across two categorical dimensions (e.g. district x indicator) and you want to surface the brightest/darkest cells at a glance. In heatmap_matrix the colour is a sequential cobalt scale (oat->signalDim->signal) and is secondary to the printed cell value.

Heatmap — use for a two-dimensional matrix, e.g. district × indicator access intensity. Renders perfectly for the medical-desert demos.

props.when_to_use: One value across two categorical axes; spot high/low cells across a matrix

nodes.name: heatmap_matrix_cobalt_sequential_scale
nodes.content: heatmap_matrix uses a SEQUENTIAL cobalt scale (P.oat -> P.signalDim -> P.signal) for cell fill, NOT the reference build_heatmap.py diverging clay->oat->olive — per the archetype spec. Colour is secondary; the printed cell number is primary.

props.statement: Cell fill = sequential cobalt scale (P.oat->P.signalDim->P.signal), not a diverging scale; colour secondary to the printed number

nodes.name: heatmap_matrix_cell_text_contrast
nodes.content: In-cell % contrast rule: use paper (light) text when v>0.45 (deep cobalt fill), else ink (dark) text. The number is primary, colour secondary, exactly as the reference intends. The mono % uses the vlabel CSS class.

props.statement: In-cell % text: paper when v>0.45 (deep cobalt fill), else ink; rendered via the vlabel mono CSS class

nodes.name: heatmap_matrix_existing_css_only
nodes.content: heatmap_matrix injects NO <style> and uses only existing CSS classes: clabel (axis labels), vlabel (in-cell mono %), annot (italic-serif spread annotation), axis/axis-title (legend).

props.statement: No injected <style>; reuse only existing classes — clabel, vlabel, annot, axis/axis-title

nodes.name: heatmap_matrix_motion_reduced_motion_safe
nodes.content: MOTION rule: every element's final geometry is set at t=0; H.in animates opacity only. The cell-group fade uses staggered delay but rects/text already carry full width/height/x/y, so a headless-Chrome screenshot at t=0 shows the complete matrix. Reduced-motion safe (H.in early-returns).

props.statement: Final geometry at t=0, opacity-only H.in fade (staggered); reduced-motion safe (H.in early-returns); screenshot at t=0 shows full matrix

nodes.name: line_multi
nodes.content: Multi-line time-series chart primitive: n named lines over a shared x-axis. The projection recipe is a sibling of line_multi (same wide viewBox 720x440) — projection extends line_multi with role-based solid/dashed styling, an uncertainty band, split-year/threshold reference lines, and crisis annotations.

props.when_to_use: Several named lines (history + projections) over a shared numeric x-axis (e.g. year); pick projection variant when extrapolation/forecast framing, uncertainty bands, or threshold crossings are needed.

nodes.name: build_nextdecade.py
nodes.content: Reference build script that the projection recipe is a faithful port of; not parsed here — referenced for provenance of the projection scene design (linear+exponential paths, band, split_year, threshold, crisis markers).

props.purpose: Reference implementation ported into the scene-engine projection recipe.

nodes.name: compose_deck
nodes.content:
Resonance Analytics deck-builder tool. Consumes ONE JSON deck spec and produces an editable PPTX with native PowerPoint charts (editable bars/lines/pies, not screenshots) plus an HTML preview from the same source of truth. Brand baked in: cobalt 'Signal' (#254BB2) + Source Serif 4 / Manrope, 1920x1080. Signature: dark cover poster (near-black field, glowing cobalt orb + amber rim) drawn automatically. No raster logos — RA brand-mark + wordmark drawn natively; there is NO `logo` field. Uploads through the Files API so bytes land in Unity Catalog and can be served back through the app (never improvise via run_python_code — that container has no UC volume mount, writes to /Volumes go to tmpfs and are silently lost). Replaces the old compose_document *pptx* path; compose_document still handles docx/xlsx/csv/pdf. Return shape JSON: {status:'ok', document_id:'deck_<12hex>', title, slide_count, preview_url:'<app>/api/decks/deck_<12hex>', pptx_url:'<app>/api/decks/deck_<12hex>.pptx', volumes_path:'/Volumes/workspace/ai_ops/agent_scratch/documents/<id>__<slug>.{pptx,html,json}', size_bytes:{pptx,html}}. preview_url/pptx_url present whenever APP_URL is configured (always in deployed app); volumes_path always returned. Always surface BOTH URLs verbatim. Errors: {status:'error', code, message} with codes empty_title/empty_spec (retry with both), no_slides (add slides), invalid_slide_type (use a known type), invalid_chart_type (use bar/column/line/pie), render_failed (strip offending slide named by index, re-call), upload_failed (UC grants issue, ping operator). Never retry the same call twice with identical args; two retries max per turn.

compose_deck(title=..., deck_spec=[slide dict, ...]) — consumes a list of slide dicts, each with a `type` field selecting the layout plus content fields. Renders the spec to PPTX (python-pptx, native charts) and HTML (preview) from the same source of truth. Slide canvas is 1920x1080 (16:9 widescreen, matches PowerPoint LAYOUT_WIDE); you don't position by pixels. Produces a Resonance Analytics-branded deck.

The compose-pptx deck-rendering tool. Defaults to the RA Signal 'Boardroom Editorial' system — the defaults baked in are correct for most decks; design.md is only needed for a specific visual brief. Sets tabular numerals in both HTML and PPTX for value columns/KPI blocks, assigns chart series colours in the legend order, draws the cover/closing orb automatically, and switches eyebrow/dash to amber + chart axes/legends to white on dark backgrounds.

compose_deck is the additive orchestrator-level tool that owns presentation decks. Signature: compose_deck(title=..., deck_spec=[...]) — one JSON spec. It renders an editable .pptx with NATIVE charts plus an HTML preview and returns preview_url + pptx_url (both URLs must be surfaced to the user). RA-branded renderer at hackathon-orchestrator/tools/compose_deck.py; the renderer draws the brand-mark and orb natively (no extra deps beyond python-pptx). Built via the factory build_compose_deck_tool(workspace_client=_workspace_client, variable_store_cls=VariableStore, app_url=APP_URL). Uploads .pptx/.html/.json to the shared documents/ volume folder. Distinct from compose_document, which keeps docx/xlsx/csv/pdf.

Orchestrator deck tool. Renders a PPTX from a deck_spec (list of slide dicts). When ra_template.pptx is present, runs in 'template mode': paints only content on top of the template's master + layouts (chrome — cover orb, logo lockup, footers, page numbers — comes from the template). Base-template resolution order (first hit wins): (1) template_path= passed to build_compose_deck_tool(...); (2) COMPOSE_DECK_TEMPLATE env var (absolute path, OR "" empty string to FORCE from-scratch mode and ignore the bundled file); (3) the bundled templates/ra_template.pptx (present now); (4) otherwise from-scratch mode. When a template is active the tool logs `[compose_deck] template mode ON, template=…` at WARNING level. From-scratch (renderer-drawn chrome) mode is fully supported and needs no template — set COMPOSE_DECK_TEMPLATE="" or delete the file. Smoke-test via tools.compose_deck._render_pptx(title, slides, template_path=...).

The compose_deck renderer draws all RA brand visuals natively in from-scratch mode — these assets are only needed when hand-building a template. In template mode the built ra_template.pptx (with embedded brand PNGs + fonts) is used and content is painted on top.

props.produces: editable PPTX with native PowerPoint charts + HTML preview; uploaded to UC volume via Files API

nodes.name: spec_reference.md
nodes.content: The full slide-type and chart-type cookbook for compose_deck. Read once per session before authoring the first deck. Most prompts only need this one read.

props.purpose: full slide-type + chart-type spec cookbook for compose_deck deck_spec authoring

nodes.name: design.md
nodes.content: The palette + type tokens reference for compose-pptx. Read only for a non-default visual brief (non-default colours / mono / monochrome). Defaults (cobalt Signal, Manrope + Source Serif 4, 1920x1080) are right for almost every deck.

props.purpose: palette + type token reference for non-default visual briefs

nodes.name: design_system_deck_example.html
nodes.content:
The canonical Resonance Analytics Design System Deck — the original 'Boardroom Editorial' system with worked examples of every layout and chart. Read it for a specific visual brief. Lives in the compose-pptx skill folder alongside tokens.css.

The canonical 36-slide Resonance Analytics design deck — every layout pattern. Read via read_file('/skills/compose-pptx/design_system_deck_example.html') when a needed layout hasn't been seen.

props.purpose: canonical 36-slide RA design deck showing every layout pattern

nodes.name: ra_template.pptx
nodes.content:
The Resonance Analytics PPTX base template that drives compose_deck 'template mode'. Ships in hackathon-orchestrator/skills/compose-pptx/templates/ (the orchestrator's skills/ dir is a symlink to hackathon-skills/, so at runtime the tool resolves it at .../hackathon-orchestrator/skills/compose-pptx/templates/ra_template.pptx). Because the file is present, compose_deck template mode is ON by default for the next deploy: the renderer paints only CONTENT on top of this template's master + layouts; backgrounds, the cobalt-orb cover, the brand-mark/wordmark, footers and page numbers all come from the template. Built reproducibly by build_ra_template.py with python-pptx, embedding brand assets from ../assets/, then calls embed_fonts.py to embed brand fonts from ../assets/fonts/. Contains 7 layouts the renderer looks up by name: RA_TitleSlide, RA_thankyou, RA_blank, RA_cream, RA_dark, RA_SectionSignal, RA_SectionWhite. Brand fonts (Source Serif 4 / Manrope / JetBrains Mono) are EMBEDDED (9 font parts + embeddedFontLst, survive the python-pptx load+save). Canvas is Inches(13.333) = 12,191,695 EMU to match compose_deck's SLIDE_W_IN coordinate system. Hand-edit warning: keep the seven layout names and idx 10/11/1 placeholders intact, and re-run embed_fonts.py afterward (hand-editing may drop embedded fonts); build_ra_template.py is the source of truth to regenerate.

ra_template.pptx is the RA-branded PowerPoint template that enables compose_deck template mode. It is dropped into hackathon-orchestrator/skills/compose-pptx/templates/ (see templates/README.md for placement). When present, the tool logs '[compose_deck] template mode ON' — the renderer paints content on top of the template chrome. When absent, the renderer draws the cobalt-orb cover and brand-mark natively. The .pptx must open with editable native charts.

Optional designed PowerPoint template for compose_deck. By default the renderer paints the full RA brand chrome itself (from-scratch mode) — no template needed. To brand from a designed template, drop it at this path. Required layout names documented in templates/README.md.

The built RA PowerPoint template that already ships, generated by build_ra_template.py. Embeds the brand PNGs (cover poster, wordmarks, geo-mark) into the slide layouts (RA_TitleSlide, RA_thankyou, content/section layouts) and has fonts embedded via embed_fonts.py. Edit the builder + re-run rather than hand-assembling from raw assets.

props.purpose: optional designed PowerPoint template (overrides from-scratch chrome rendering)

nodes.name: compose-pptx-templates-readme
nodes.content: Documents the required layout names for the optional ra_template.pptx template used by compose_deck.

props.purpose: required layout names for the optional ra_template.pptx

nodes.name: cover
nodes.content:
`cover` — first slide. Fields: title (hero text Source Serif 4 display ~72pt, <=8 words), lede (one-line subtitle, <=24 words), eyebrow. Example: {"type":"cover","bg":"ink","eyebrow":"DAIS FOR GOOD · 2026","title":"The Care Lottery","lede":"Why a district's access to healthcare varies up to 80x across India."}. Default bg is "ink" — the dark cobalt-orb poster (the RA signature). Use bg:"signal" for a solid-cobalt cover, or bg:"paper" for a light cover, only with a reason. The RA brand-mark + wordmark are drawn automatically; no `logo` field.

First slide of the deck. Deck title + lede on a dark cobalt-orb poster (ink field, glowing cobalt orb + amber rim, drawn automatically). Title gets Source Serif 4 (display). Keep title short (<=8 words, fits two lines); push period/scope/preparer detail into `lede`. One of the dark slides that sandwich the deck (open dark, close dark).

Cover slide. Anchored on cobalt (bg:signal) or the dark ink poster (bg:ink); gets the brand-mark + wordmark and the auto-drawn orb. Hero uses Source Serif 4 display-xl 128px (or display-l 88px for a large cover). Cover/closing titles may be centered. On dark bg the eyebrow + dash are amber.

Deck cover (slide 1, the kept seed slide). In template mode routes to layout RA_TitleSlide: renderer fills placeholder idx 10 = title (Source Serif) and idx 1 = subtitle; the layout supplies the full-bleed cover-poster.png orb, the RA logo lockup top-left, and the page number. Uses the top-left logo lockup with no footer lockup (hero treatment).

props.when_to_use: First slide; deck title + lede (dark cobalt-orb poster)

nodes.name: section_divider
nodes.content:
`section_divider` — major-act divider. Fields: title, lede, eyebrow, bg. Example: {"type":"section_divider","bg":"signal","eyebrow":"SECTION · 02","title":"Recommendations","lede":"Three levers."}. Use ONCE per major act. Don't use as a "spacer" between content. `signal` (cobalt) or `ink` (dark) both work; `paper` for a light divider. RA brand-mark + wordmark drawn automatically.

Opens a new act/section ('Findings', 'Risks', 'Recommendations'). Section title gets Source Serif 4 (display). Can go signal (cobalt) background. Keep section titles short (<=8 words).

Section divider slide. Anchors on cobalt (bg:signal) and carries the brand-mark + wordmark. Section title uses Source Serif 4 display-m 64px. On the cobalt/dark background the eyebrow + dash are amber.

Section divider slide. In template mode the bg key selects the layout: bg:'signal' → RA_SectionSignal (solid cobalt #17368D fill, logo lockup top-left, footer); bg:'paper' or 'white' → RA_SectionWhite (subtle blue #F9FAFC design-system paper fill, ink lockup top-left, footer). The renderer fills an eyebrow + serif title + lede. NOTE bg:'signal' is section-divider ONLY — on a content slide it falls back to RA_blank (white).

props.when_to_use: Open a new act ('Findings', 'Risks', 'Recommendations')

nodes.name: stat_callout
nodes.content:
`stat_callout` — single hero number. Fields: value (the number, renders ~130pt Source Serif in cobalt Signal, use K/M/B/x suffixes — don't dump 12 digits), unit (small label after value e.g. "%","M","districts"), delta (optional change figure e.g. "+12.4%","Bihar vs Kerala"), delta_dir ("up"=green/"down"=red/"flat"=slate), caption (one-line interpretation — the number alone is not the insight), title, eyebrow. Example: {"type":"stat_callout","eyebrow":"HEADLINE","title":"Zero-facility districts","value":"245","unit":"districts","delta":"of ~700 NFHS districts","delta_dir":"down","caption":"Districts with no facility recorded in the ~10k-facility sample — coverage, not census."}. ONE stat_callout per deck max — every other number lives in a kpi_grid or chart. MUST have a caption.

Single hero number — the deck's headline metric, in cobalt Source Serif 4 (display). Only ONE display-size number per slide; everything else is a kpi_grid or chart. Inline-format the value directly in the spec (e.g. value '245', unit 'districts') — don't chain queries to pre-format.

Stat callout slide — the single headline number of the deck (cobalt serif). Only ONE stat_callout per deck. The big-stat value is centered, in Source Serif 4 (display-l/xl), with K/M/B suffix if large, tabular numerals. Ends with a one-line caption (scope, period, interpretation). All other numbers go in kpi_grid/table/chart.

props.when_to_use: Single hero number — the deck's headline metric (cobalt serif)

nodes.name: kpi_grid
nodes.content:
`kpi_grid` — 3-4 supporting metrics. Fields: kpis (list of {label, value, unit, delta, delta_dir}), title, eyebrow, caption. Example: {"type":"kpi_grid","eyebrow":"AT A GLANCE","title":"Four signals","kpis":[{"label":"DISTRICTS","value":"700","unit":"","delta":"NFHS-5","delta_dir":"flat"},{"label":"ZERO-FACILITY","value":"245","unit":"","delta":"~35%","delta_dir":"down"},{"label":"FACILITIES","value":"10","unit":"k","delta":"sample","delta_dir":"flat"},{"label":"WORST HBI","value":"Bihar","unit":"","delta":"vs Kerala","delta_dir":"down"}],"caption":"India, NFHS-5 + Virtue Foundation facility sample."}. Best with exactly 3 or 4 tiles; <=6 max. Each tile is a rounded card with a cobalt accent tick, uppercase label, and a Source Serif value. MUST have a meaningful title + eyebrow (caption optional in lieu).

3-4 supporting metrics shown in tiles. KPI values get Source Serif 4 (display). Use for the supporting metrics around the one hero stat_callout.

KPI grid slide — a grid of KPI tiles. KPI values are set in Source Serif 4 (display moment) with tabular numerals; tiles use 32px card padding. Don't repeat kpi_grid ×5 (reads as filler) — vary with stat_callout/chart/two_column/quote. Holds the secondary numbers that aren't the deck's one headline stat_callout. Caption names scope/period/interpretation.

props.when_to_use: 3-4 supporting metrics in tiles

nodes.name: bullets
nodes.content:
`bullets` — title + lede + bullet list. Fields: title, lede, bullets (list of strings), eyebrow. Example: {"type":"bullets","eyebrow":"RECOMMENDATIONS","title":"Three things to do this quarter","lede":"Ranked by expected impact.","bullets":["Prioritise the 245 zero-facility districts for siting.","Validate self-reported specialty claims before relying on them.","Publish an open district access-gap dashboard."]}. Don't use as a default. ALWAYS prefer a chart, KPI grid, or stat callout where the data fits. Bullets are the lowest-effort slide and read that way.

Plain content slide with title + lede + bullet list. Use SPARINGLY (plain bullets on white won't impress; text-only slides are forgettable — add a visual element). Left-align lists, never center body text. Titles and body are Manrope.

props.when_to_use: Plain content with title + lede + bullet list (use sparingly)

nodes.name: two_column
nodes.content:
`two_column` — side-by-side comparison. Fields: left and right (each {heading, bullets}), title, eyebrow. Example: {"type":"two_column","eyebrow":"FINDINGS · 02","title":"What the data can and cannot say","left":{"heading":"We can show","bullets":["Where facilities are recorded","District NFHS-5 health burden","The access gap by district"]},"right":{"heading":"We cannot claim","bullets":["True supply (sample, not census)","Per-capita rates (no population)","Verified specialty availability"]}}. Equal-width columns. <=4 bullets per side.

Two-column layout for paired content: pros/cons, before/after, 'what we can / cannot say'.

Two-column slide. Left-aligned lists/ledes (never center body text). Part of the varied rhythm (stat_callout → chart → kpi_grid → two_column → quote). Carries the brand via chrome (eyebrow + dash + page number), not a repeated brand-mark.

props.when_to_use: Pros/cons, before/after, what we can / cannot say

nodes.name: chart
nodes.content:
`chart` — NATIVE PowerPoint chart. Fields: chart ("column" vertical / "bar" horizontal / "line" / "pie"), categories (x-axis or pie slice labels), series (list of {name, values} — ONE series for pie, up to 6 for column/bar/line), value_label (value-axis title, rendered in PPTX for column/bar/line, ignored for pie and in HTML preview, "" to omit), caption (one-line interpretation, always include), show_values (True default shows data labels on column/bar/pie; line charts never show point labels), show_legend (False default for single-series, True if 2+ series), title, eyebrow. Example: {"type":"chart","eyebrow":"CHARTS · 01","title":"Facility coverage by state","chart":"column","categories":["Kerala","Tamil Nadu","Maharashtra","Uttar Pradesh","Bihar"],"series":[{"name":"Facilities","values":[820,610,540,330,190]}],"value_label":"count (sample)","caption":"Virtue Foundation ~10k facility sample. Bihar records the fewest despite a high health burden — coverage, not census.","show_values":true,"show_legend":false}. Each series[*].values length MUST match categories length. MUST have a caption.

Native PowerPoint bar/column/line/pie chart with data labels (editable PowerPoint chart objects). `chart` field must be one of bar/column/line/pie. <=6 series per chart — beyond that the palette runs out (split into multiple charts or use a stacked treatment). Lead series in cobalt. Title says what the data says ('Bihar records the fewest facilities'), not the chart kind ('Bar chart of facility coverage').

Chart slide. Uses the chart palette legend order (cobalt → amber → cyan → magenta → light-cobalt → slate), ≤6 series. On dark backgrounds (bg:ink for dramatic charts) axes + legends turn white. Ends with a one-line caption (scope, period, interpretation). Chart axes/tables may keep precise numbers (K/M/B only on display values).

props.when_to_use: Native PowerPoint bar/column/line/pie with data labels

nodes.name: chart_commentary
nodes.content:
`chart_commentary` — chart + sidebar bullets. Fields: chart/categories/series/value_label/show_legend (same as chart), commentary (list of {label, text}, 3-4 items max — each is a bold cobalt (or amber on dark) label + a short paragraph), title, eyebrow, bg. Example: {"type":"chart_commentary","bg":"ink","eyebrow":"ANALYSIS · 02","title":"Coverage does not track need","chart":"line","categories":["T1 (low burden)","T2","T3 (high burden)"],"series":[{"name":"Facilities (raw)","values":[12,9,6]},{"name":"Adjusted for urbanisation","values":[9,9,9]}],"value_label":"facilities/district","show_legend":true,"commentary":[{"label":"Raw gap","text":"High-burden districts look under-served."},{"label":"Confounder","text":"The gap is urbanisation, not burden."},{"label":"Honest null","text":"Adjusted, the effect is not significant."}]}. Chart ~60% width, commentary ~40%. On a dark (ink) background the chart axes + legend auto-switch to white. The tool is forgiving about commentary shape — a list of {label,text} is canonical, but a {title,bullets} dict or a bare list of strings is coerced automatically. MUST have a caption.

A chart plus a sidebar of 3-4 short observations.

props.when_to_use: Chart + sidebar of 3-4 short observations

nodes.name: table
nodes.content:
`table` — tabular data. Fields: headers (list), rows (list of cell lists), title, eyebrow, caption. Example: {"type":"table","eyebrow":"DATA · 01","title":"Highest-burden districts with zero facilities","headers":["District","State","HBI"],"rows":[["Araria","Bihar","94.9"],["Kishanganj","Bihar","88.2"],["Sheohar","Bihar","86.0"]],"caption":"NFHS-5 health-burden index; no facility recorded in the ~10k sample. Header row is cobalt; numeric columns right-aligned with tabular numerals."}. <=6 rows x <=5 cols. The tool right-aligns columns whose header contains a unit or % (recognises %, USD, EUR, GBP, (M), Δ). MUST have a caption.

Tabular data, capped at <=6 rows x <=5 cols. Table headers are cobalt (Signal).

props.when_to_use: Tabular data <=6 rows x <=5 cols

nodes.name: quote
nodes.content:
`quote` — pull-quote breather. Fields: quote (text, Source Serif 4 italic ~40pt), attribution, caption, eyebrow, bg. Example: {"type":"quote","bg":"cream","eyebrow":"VOICE · FIELD","quote":"The nearest hospital is a day's travel — if you can travel.","attribution":"Community health worker","caption":"Recurring theme in high-burden rural districts."}. A breather between dense slides. `cream` (warm) or `paper` work well.

Pull-quote slide, set in Source Serif 4 italic (display). Quotes may be centered (one of the few centered elements allowed).

Quote / pull-quote slide. Often on cream background (bg:cream, warm #FBF0E4) as a breather. Pull-quotes use italic Source Serif 4; quote text is centered. Part of the varied slide rhythm.

props.when_to_use: Pull-quote (Source Serif italic)

nodes.name: data_note
nodes.content:
`data_note` — required when substituting periods/scope or surfacing a data caveat. Fields: requested, available, reason, title, eyebrow. Example: {"type":"data_note","eyebrow":"DATA NOTE","title":"Scope","requested":"True facility supply per district","available":"~10,000-facility sample (coverage)","reason":"The facilities table is a sample, not a census, and has no population denominator — read it as coverage, not supply or per-capita."}. ALWAYS the second slide (right after cover) when the user asked for something the data can't fully support. Don't bury it later.

Period / scope substitution or data caveat slide. When requested data is unavailable or only a sample, the FIRST content slide MUST be a data_note naming the requested vs available scope and the reason.

props.when_to_use: Period / scope substitution or data caveat

nodes.name: timeline
nodes.content:
`timeline` — sequenced steps. Fields: steps (list of {label, heading, body}), title, eyebrow. Example: {"type":"timeline","eyebrow":"PLAN · NEXT 90 DAYS","title":"Roll-out cadence","steps":[{"label":"Q1","heading":"Rank deserts","body":"Score every district on the access-gap index."},{"label":"Q2","heading":"Validate claims","body":"Audit self-reported specialty availability."},{"label":"Q3","heading":"Publish","body":"Ship the open district access dashboard."}]}. 3-5 steps. Each is a cobalt rail label + bold heading + body sentence.

Process steps or milestone progression laid out as a timeline.

props.when_to_use: Process steps or milestone progression

nodes.name: closing
nodes.content:
`closing` — final slide. Fields: title, lede, eyebrow, bg, motif.
